Using the Report Wizard window</strong></p><p>This User Interface model can now be exported as an image. The steps to do this are as follows:</p><ol><li>Select <strong>Save As Image</strong> from the <strong>File</strong> menu.</li><li>In the Save As Image dialog, select <strong>Active Diagram</strong>.</li><li>In <strong>Image File</strong> define the location where the image should be placed.</li><li>Select <em>Joint Photographic Experts Group (.jpg)</em> (or any format you would like) in <strong>Image Format</strong> and then click Save.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="90fee36a-0a2e-4da5-9773-55a3c3df239c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">982345345897</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cb43bef2-6b43-4a53-97ba-d5789e0dafe6"><ac:parameter ac:name="">CaseStudy2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Case Study 2 - Slider Example</h3><p>This case study contains step-by-step instructions for creating a User Interface model with Sliders. It also shows how to customize the symbol properties of User Interface components. However, it does <strong>not</strong> explain how to create a new project and a new diagram. Please refer to &quot;<ac:link ac:anchor="CaseStudy1"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Case Study 1 - Modeling user interface for the Report Wizard dialog]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>&quot; for those instructions. <span> If you are already familiar with these case studies, please review</span><span> </span><ac:link ac:anchor="CaseStudy3"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Case Study 3 - User Interface Prototyping Example]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p><p><strong>Step #1 Create a Frame, Labels and Sliders</strong></p><ol><li>Create a new <strong>Frame</strong>.<ol><li>Under the <strong>Containers</strong> toolbar, click the <strong>Frame</strong> button.</li><li>Drop it on the diagram pane.</li><li>Click on your newly created frame and title <em>Symbol Properties Customization.</em></li></ol></li><li>Add a new <strong>Label</strong> to the Frame.<ol><li>Under the <strong>Tools</strong> toolbar, click the <strong>Label</strong> button. Place the label onto the frame.</li><li>Click the label and change the name to <em>Fill Color.</em></li><li>Create the remaining two labels and name them <em>Text Color</em> and <em>Font Name</em>.</li></ol></li><li>Add <strong>Sliders</strong> to the Frame.<ol><li>Under the <strong>Other</strong> toolbar, click the <strong>Slider</strong> button. Place the slider onto the frame.</li><li>Double-click on the Slider to invoke its specification window.</li><li>In the opened window, set the <strong>Spacing</strong> property value to 50.</li><li>Set the <strong>Knob Position</strong> property value to 0 in order to move the knob to the left position.</li><li>In the Slider's specification window, select the <strong>Values</strong> property and click the plus sign button. A Values dialog window opens. If there are values already defined in the window, click the minus sign button to delete them. </li><li>To add new values, click the plus sign button and then type <em>0 Red</em>. <em>0 Red</em> must be written in a specific way. First, type in <em>0</em> and press <strong>Enter</strong>. Type <em>Red </em>on the next line. Rename the other two given values to <em>50 Blue</em> and <em>100 White</em> using the same procedure.</li><li>Draw two more sliders and repeat steps 3a - 3f until your frame looks like the one shown in the figure below.</li></ol></li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8625b9f4-ffb5-4447-9e18-e673dc79fc66"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Notation Formality</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When setting the values of a slider it is important to note that there is no empty space between <em>0</em>. For example, <em>0</em> represents a new line. When entering a value, it should look like this:</p><p><ac:image ac:align="left" ac:width="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2017-07-22 at 1.03.50 PM.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h6 style="text-align: center;"><br /><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="256" ac:width="501"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav53d3c9355ce1fa8f8aeb943c85e060d5.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> Symbol Properties Customization Frame with Added Labels and Sliders</h6><p><strong>Step #2 Add Text Fields</strong></p><ol><li>Create Text Fields.<ol><li>Under the <strong>Text</strong> toolbar. click the <strong>Text Field</strong> button. Drop it onto the frame. </li><li>Name the text field <em>Red Background</em>.</li><li>Repeat the previous two steps for the remaining two text fields, as shown in the figure below.</li></ol></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="255" ac:width="501"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav0212b54c35d95fb5cb12d97d73b2c6a3.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Added Text Fields to the Frame</h6><p><strong>Step #3 Edit Symbol Properties for the Text Fields</strong></p><ol><li>Edit <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> for the first text field.<ol><li>Select the first text field on the Frame shape.</li><li>Right-click it and select <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> from its shortcut menu.</li><li>Set the <strong>Use Fill Color</strong> property to true and change the <strong>Fill Color</strong> property value to red. Change the fill color by clicking the ... to the right of the field, and in the Color dialog, click <strong>RGB </strong>and type in <em>FF0000</em> in the <strong>Color Code</strong> field.</li></ol></li><li>Edit <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> for the remaining fields.<ol><li>Follow steps 1a - 1b for the next steps.</li><li>For the second text field, select yellow in <strong>Text Color</strong>.</li><li>For the third one, select the font name Tahoma in <strong>Font</strong>, as shown in the figure below </li></ol></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="257" ac:width="500"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav4a6ff3bdd67c8337921149a2d11507f0.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Finalized Slider Example</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="83028fde-0aa2-472a-aac9-b0d6acc6c427"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">458973459054</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="223a7361-ed93-4ecd-b934-a2513a86262c"><ac:parameter ac:name="">CaseStudy3</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Case Study 3 - User Interface Prototyping Example</h3><p>This case study demonstrates how to connect several user interface models and create a browsable report. The models should represent a test application with a Login Dialog, a Test Browser and a test with several questions. Here are step-by-step instructions on how to add hyperlinks and create browsable reports. <span>If you are already familiar with this case study, please review </span><ac:link ac:anchor="CaseStudy2"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Case Study 2 - Slider Example]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span>, or <ac:link ac:anchor="CaseStudy1"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Case Study 1 - Modeling user interface for the Report Wizard dialog]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></p><p><strong>Step #1 Create the first Model</strong></p><ol><li><p><span>Create a new Package for the model.</span></p></li><li><p><span>Create a model similar to the one shown below.</span></p></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="179" ac:width="318"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav0c437b6b5c5cf1161007d0bd842f87e7.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);">First Model of Prototyping Example - Login Dialog</span></h6><p><strong>Step #2 Create the second Model</strong></p><ol><li><p>Create another new Package for this model.</p></li><li><p>Create a model similar to the one depicted below.</p></li></ol><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:height="238" ac:width="488"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavc6cb8ac9da4f9b8f2fe40099cf5f9e86.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Case Studies for User Interface Modeling" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Second Model of Prototyping Example - Test Browser</h6><p><strong>Step #3 Create remaining Models</strong></p><ol><li><p>Create a separate Package for each model.</p></li><li><p>You can build models similar to the ones shown in the detailed samples mentioned in the beginning of this chapter, or create your own in a similar manner.</p></li></ol><p><strong>Step #4 Add Hyperlinks</strong></p><ol><li><p>Add a hyperlink.</p><ol><li><p>Open the model with the Login Dialog.</p></li><li><p>Open the Specification window of the <strong>OK</strong> button.</p></li><li>Next to the Active Hyperlink property field, click the ... button. The <strong>Edit Hyperlink</strong> dialog opens.</li><li>In the Edit Hyperlink dialog, select <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Element/Symbol</strong> and click the &quot;...&quot; button. Browse to the Package <strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Test Browser</strong>, select the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) User Interface Modeling diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[User Interface diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in it and confirm two times with OK - a diagram symbol will appear next to the OK button. Double-clicking the OK button will lead to the other diagram.</li></ol></li><li><p>Now connect all the buttons in the other diagrams with hyperlinks. The diagram symbol appears next to a component with a hyperlink.</p></li></ol><p><strong>Step #5 Create a browsable Report</strong></p><ol><li><p>Create a new report.</p><ol><li><p>Select <strong>Report Wizard</strong> from the <strong>Tools</strong> menu.</p></li><li><p>Expand the <strong>Default Template</strong> in the tree and select <strong>Web Publisher 2.0</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Confirm three times by clicking <strong>Next</strong> until the dialog allows you to add data to the report. Add all Packages that contain the created diagrams and click <strong>Next</strong>.</p></li><li><p>Give the <strong>Report file</strong> a name to the output file. Clear the check box <strong>Display in viewer after generating report</strong>. </p></li><li><p>Click <strong>Generate</strong>. The report is built and shown in your default browser.</p></li></ol></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6420b013-e83a-4c61-852d-115bb9202c8f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">433458345987534</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related References:</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) UML diagrams" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Extension diagrams" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Extension diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="7450a3ef-4b10-43be-b787-2410909608bb"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) User Interface Modeling diagram" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272739907 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02521: (2026x) Case Study

- page_id: `272739907`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272739907/2026x+Case+Study
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:52:12.394+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) Procedures > (2026x) Displaying information indirectly related to element through Derived Properties
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Let’s say, we need to show Warning Order and Medical Condition exchange elements that are produced and consumed by the performer Search Node. Exchange elements are information indirectly related to the Search Node element.

We will analyze the OV-2 diagram from the DoDAF sample project.

First, we will show the exchange elements produced by the Search Node.

Let’s create the **Produces Exchange Element** derived property for the performer *Search Node*. Open the *Search Node* **Specification** window and in the **Traceability** property group click the **Create** button. In the opened **Criterion Editor** dialog, select the **Meta Chain** tab. Then specify the derived property name and the expression for the meta chain as it is shown in the following figure.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Specification of derived property name and expression

In the following figure, you can see that a new subgroup named **Other** is created in the performer **Specification**window. Our created **Produces Exchange Element** derived property is placed under this subgroup. The **Produces Exchange Element** derived property has *Warning Order* and *Medical Condition* exchange elements as its values.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of newly created derived property

Next, we need to display values of the **Produces Exchange Element** derived property for the *Search Node* performer in the OV-2 diagram.

A derived property is applicable to an element type, not the specific element. In this particular example, our created derived properties are applicable to all performers, not only to*Search Node*.

We can use a Note element wherein we can display information indirectly related to the *Search Node*. Let’s draw a Note and connect it to the Search Node performer. Then click the Edit Compartment as it is shown in the following figure. In the **Compartment Edit** dialog, select the **Element Properties** tab. From the properties list, select to display the **Produces Exchange Element** property. After clicking the **OK** button, *Warning Order* and *Medical Condition* exchange elements are displayed in the Note.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Information indirectly related to Search Node is displayed in Note

Now, let’s create a generic table wherein we can also display information indirectly related to the *Search Node*, as well as to other performers. For that purpose, we select Performer as an **Element Type** and add existing performers to the table. Then we select which information to display as new columns. Previously created **Produces Exchange Element** derived property is also available to add as a new column through the **Show Columns** menu (see the following figure).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Produces Exchange Element derived property displayed as column

Now, let’s create a new derived property directly in the generic table. This property will show which exchange elements are consumed by the performer element. We can do that using the same **Criterion Editor** and doing the same actions as described previously. In the following figure is the example of the **Consumes Exchange Element** derived property expression.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Consumes Exchange Element derived property expression

When the expression is specified, the newly created derived property is displayed as a new column (see the following figure).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Consumes Exchange Element derived property added as new column

#### PANEL: Related procedure

Related procedure

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying information indirectly related to element through Derived Properties' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

In this particular example, our created derived properties are applicable to all performers, not only to<em> Search Node</em>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>We can use a Note element wherein we can display information indirectly related to the <em>Search Node</em>. Let’s draw a Note and connect it to the Search Node performer. Then click the Edit Compartment as it is shown in the following figure. In the <strong>Compartment Edit</strong> dialog, select the <strong>Element Properties</strong> tab. From the properties list, select to display the <strong>Produces Exchange Element</strong> property. After clicking the <strong>OK</strong> button, <em>Warning Order</em> and <em>Medical Condition</em> exchange elements are displayed in the Note.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="case_study_III.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Case Study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Information indirectly related to Search Node is displayed in Note</h6><p>Now, let’s create a generic table wherein we can also display information indirectly related to the <em>Search Node</em>, as well as to other performers. Previously created <strong>Produces Exchange Element</strong> derived property is also available to add as a new column through the <strong>Show Columns</strong> menu (see the following figure).</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="case_study_IV.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Case Study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Produces Exchange Element derived property displayed as column</h6><p>Now, let’s create a new derived property directly in the generic table. This property will show which exchange elements are consumed by the performer element. We can do that using the same <strong>Criterion Editor</strong> and doing the same actions as described previously. In the following figure is the example of the <strong>Consumes Exchange Element</strong> derived property expression.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="case_study_V.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Case Study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Consumes Exchange Element derived property expression</h6><p>When the expression is specified, the newly created derived property is displayed as a new column (see the following figure).</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="case_study_VI.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Case Study" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Consumes Exchange Element derived property added as new column</h6></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="56056506-16fd-4b38-a100-47b4499cf79e"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related procedure</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Displaying information indirectly related to element through Derived Properties" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272730841 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02523: (2026x) Change impact analysis

- page_id: `272730841`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272730841/2026x+Change+impact+analysis
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:37:41.598+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x) Requirements management > (2026x) Analyzing Requirements
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Impact analysis minimizes the risk of introducing undesirable effects to the system by increasing your understanding of how the proposed change might affect the elements in the system change. Once a change is proposed its impact should be identified.

Traceability links and visualization of related elements display dependencies of requirements and help you see the full impact of a proposed change in those requirements.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Impact analysis minimizes the risk of introducing undesirable effects to the system by increasing your understanding of how the proposed change might affect the elements in the system change. Once a change is proposed its impact should be identified.</p><p>Traceability links and visualization of related elements display dependencies of requirements and help you see the full impact of a proposed change in those requirements.</p><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272738739 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02524: (2026x) Changed Property Types

- page_id: `272738739`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738739/2026x+Changed+Property+Types
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:50:31.359+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) Migration User Guides > (2026x) UPDM projects migration manual > (2026x) UPDM 2.1 migration to UAF 1.1 > (2026x) Mapping issues and solutions
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### Changes for Condition and Environment

In earlier versions, the Activity Performable Under Condition property (owned by Activity) had the Condition as its value. After the migration, the newly created Actual Condition with the Condition set as a type, replaces this property.

The same is valid for the Required Environment (owned by Location Holder) value. If the value was Environment, the Actual Environment is created with Environment set to this property. If the value was Condition, it is removed and added to ToDo property.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Changes for Doctrine

In earlier versions the Doctrine property (owned by Capability Configuration) had Constraint for its value. In UAF 1.1 Constraint is replaced by Standard Operational Activity. After the migration, Capability Configuration is set as constrainedElement to Constraint Except if Constraint is Operational Constraint or Service Policy.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h4>Changes for Condition and Environment</h4><p>In earlier versions, the Activity Performable Under Condition property (owned by Activity) had the Condition as its value. After the migration, the newly created Actual Condition with the Condition set as a type, replaces this property. </p><p>The same is valid for the Required Environment (owned by Location Holder) value. I<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">f the value was Environment, the Actual Environment is created with Environment set to this property. If the value was Condition, it is removed and added to ToDo property.</span></p><p><br /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="concition_to_instance.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Changed Property Types" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h4>Changes for Doctrine</h4><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">In earlier versions the Doctrine property (owned by Capability Configuration) had Constraint for its value. In UAF 1.1 Constraint is replaced by Standard Operational Activity. After the migration, Capability Configuration is set as constrainedElement to Constraint Except if Constraint is Operational Constraint or Service Policy.</span></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272738686 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02525: (2026x) Changed Property Types in UAF 1.2

- page_id: `272738686`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738686/2026x+Changed+Property+Types+in+UAF+1.2
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:50:28.492+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) Migration User Guides > (2026x) UPDM projects migration manual > (2026x) UPDM 2.1 migration to UAF 1.2 > (2026x) UPDM 2.1 to UAF 1.2. Mapping issues and solutions
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### Changes for Condition and Environment

In earlier versions, the Activity Performable Under Condition property (owned by Activity) had the Condition as its value. After the migration, the newly created Actual Condition with the Condition set as a type, replaces this property.

The same is valid for the Required Environment (owned by Location Holder) value. If the value was Environment, the Actual Environment is created with Environment set to this property. If the value was Condition, it is removed and added to ToDo property.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Changes for Doctrine

In earlier versions the Doctrine property (owned by Capability Configuration) had Constraint for its value. In UAF 1.3 Constraint is replaced by Standard Operational Activity. After the migration, Capability Configuration is set as constrainedElement to Constraint Except if Constraint is Operational Constraint or Service Policy.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h4>Changes for Condition and Environment</h4><p>In earlier versions, the Activity Performable Under Condition property (owned by Activity) had the Condition as its value. After the migration, the newly created Actual Condition with the Condition set as a type, replaces this property. </p><p>The same is valid for the Required Environment (owned by Location Holder) value. I<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">f the value was Environment, the Actual Environment is created with Environment set to this property. If the value was Condition, it is removed and added to ToDo property.</span></p><p><br /><span class="confluence-embedded-file-wrapper"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="concition_to_instance.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="Changed Property Types in UAF 1.3" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h4>Changes for Doctrine</h4><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">In earlier versions the Doctrine property (owned by Capability Configuration) had Constraint for its value. In UAF 1.3 Constraint is replaced by Standard Operational Activity. After the migration, Capability Configuration is set as constrainedElement to Constraint Except if Constraint is Operational Constraint or Service Policy.</span></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272738701 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02526: (2026x) Changes for Security Control

- page_id: `272738701`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738701/2026x+Changes+for+Security+Control
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:50:29.108+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) Migration User Guides > (2026x) UAF projects migration manual > (2026x) Migration from UPDM 3 (UAF 1.0 beta) to UAF 1.1 > (2026x) Migration from UPDM 3 (UAF 1.0 beta) to UAF 1.0
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The new approach for modeling the Security Control (and its specialization) is based on [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Requirement' space='MED']. The profile changes are described in the following table:

| Before migration | After migration | Comment |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Name | Type | Name | Type |  |
| Security Control | Activity | Security Control | Class | Aditionally, Security Process is created to overtake the information, that cannot be stored in Security Control [Class]. |
| Security Control Family | Comment | Security Control Family | Class | The Criterion from SecurityControlFamily [comment] is moved to the Text of the SecurityControlFamily [class, requirement].Information about the Annotated Element is moved to ToDo. |
| Enhanced Security Control | Activity | Enhanced Security Control | Class | Aditionally, Security Process is created to overtake the information, that cannot be stored in Security Control [Class]. |
| Security Control Action | Call Behavior Action | Security Process Action | Call Behavior Action |  |
| - | - | Security Process | Activity | Security Process is a new element dedicated for modeling security behaviors. |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

The profile changes are described in the following table:</p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 64.1848%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 18.1356%;" /><col style="width: 13.8983%;" /><col <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272738684 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02528: (2026x) Changes in properties and metaclasses UAF 1.2

- page_id: `272738684`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738684/2026x+Changes+in+properties+and+metaclasses+UAF+1.2
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:50:28.310+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) Migration User Guides > (2026x) UPDM projects migration manual > (2026x) UPDM 2.1 migration to UAF 1.2 > (2026x) UPDM 2.1 to UAF 1.2. Mapping issues and solutions
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Enduring Task removed

Enduring Task element was removed and after the migration it become Strategic Phase.

##### Desired Effect changes

Desired Effect renamed to Desires. After the migration relationship is removed because the supplier has changed (from Desired State to Actual State).

##### Changes for properties of Actual Enterprise Phase

- Vision property was removed, so after the migration Phases relationship was created between Actual Enterprise Phase and Enterprise Vision
- Goal property was removed, so after the migration Phases relationship was created between Actual Enterprise Phase and Enterprise Goal

##### Changes for property of *Enterprise Goal*

- Enterprise Phase property was removed, so after the migration Phases relationship was created between Actual Enterprise Phase and Enterprise Goal

##### Changes for *property of* *Enterprise Vision*

- Enterprise Phase property was removed, so after the migration Phases relationship was created between Actual Enterprise Phase and Enterprise Vision

##### Changed Property Types

In earlier versions, the Benefit property (owned by Enterprise Goal) was String. In UAF 1.3 Benefit property has Value Item for its value.After migration, Value Item element will be created based on Benefit value.

##### Changed for Whole Life Enterprise

In earlier version, WholeLieEneterprise was Class. In UAF 1.3 WholeLieEneterprise will be Instance Specification. After migration, Whole Life Enterprise becomes Strategic Phase.

**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h3>Enduring Task removed</h3><p>Enduring Task element was removed and after the migration it become Strategic Phase. </p><h3>Desired Effect changes</h3><p>Desired Effect renamed to Desires. After the migration relationship is removed because the supplier has changed (from Desired State to Actual State).</p><h3><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="bac8e30d-4e38-434c-8fb0-f4002fd53aba">Changes for properties of  Actual Enterprise Phase</ac:inline-comment-marker></h3><ul><li><strong>Vision</strong> property was removed, so after the migration <em>Phases</em> relationship was created between <em>Actual Enterprise Phase</em> and <em>Enterprise Vision</em></li><li><strong>Goal</strong> property was removed, so after the migration <em>Phases</em> relationship was created between <em>Actual Enterprise Phase</em> and <em>Enterprise Goal</em></li></ul><h3>Changes for property of <em><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Enterprise Goal</span></em></h3><ul><li><strong>Enterprise Phase </strong>property was removed, so after the migration <em>Phases</em> relationship was created between <em>Actual Enterprise Phase</em> and <em>Enterprise Goal</em></li></ul><h3>Changes for <em style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">property of </span></em> <em style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;"><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">Enterprise Vision </span></em></h3><ul><li><strong>Enterprise Phase </strong>property was removed, so after the migration <em>Phases</em> relationship was created between <em>Actual Enterprise Phase</em> and <em>Enterprise Vision</em></li></ul><h3><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="230554ab-0e82-49cc-86d0-15b6d571d8e6">Changed Property Types</ac:inline-comment-marker></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">In earlier versions, the Benefit property (owned by Enterprise Goal) was String. In UAF 1.3  Benefit property <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">has Value Item for its value. </span>After migration, Value Item element will be created based on Benefit value. </span></p><h3><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c23d762c-2384-4095-9a5f-a424f761adb7">Changed for Whole Life Enterprise</ac:inline-comment-marker></h3><p>In earlier version, WholeLieEneterprise was Class. In UAF 1.3 WholeLieEneterprise  will be Instance Specification. After migration, Whole Life Enterprise becomes Strategic Phase. </p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br /></span></p><p><em><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);"><br /></span></em></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272738709 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02529: (2026x) Changes in Security Traceability matrices

- page_id: `272738709`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738709/2026x+Changes+in+Security+Traceability+matrices
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:50:29.699+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) Migration User Guides > (2026x) UAF projects migration manual > (2026x) Migration from UPDM 3 (UAF 1.0 beta) to UAF 1.1 > (2026x) Migration from UPDM 3 (UAF 1.0 beta) to UAF 1.0
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Together with the UAF 1.0 profile specification release, the following changes were implemented:

| UAF 1.0 beta | UAF 1.0 |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Old matrix | Client / Supplier | New matrix | Client / Supplier |
| Security Affect Traceability [Deprecated] | Risk / Asset Role | Risks to Assets Mapping | Risk / Asset |
| Security Mitigation Traceability [Deprecated] | Asset Role / Risk | Security Controls to Risks Mapping | Security Control / Risk |

The old matrices are not migrated, although they are left in the model. To continue modeling the Security Traceability matrices, please create the new ones.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target">Together with the UAF 1.0 profile specification release, the following changes were implemented:</p><table><colgroup><col /><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th style="text-align: center;" colspan="2">UAF 1.0 beta</th><th style="text-align: center;" colspan="2">UAF 1.0</th></tr><tr><th style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">Old matrix</th><th style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">Client / Supplier</th><th style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">New matrix</th><th style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">Client / Supplier</th></tr><tr><td>Security Affect Traceability [Deprecated]</td><td colspan="1">Risk / Asset Role</td><td>Risks to Assets Mapping</td><td colspan="1">Risk / Asset</td></tr><tr><td>Security Mitigation Traceability [Deprecated]</td><td colspan="1">Asset Role / Risk</td><td>Security Controls to Risks Mapping</td><td colspan="1">Security Control / Risk</td></tr></tbody></table><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a032a32f-34b8-467d-9c29-d11916a4940a"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The old matrices are not migrated, although they are left in the model. To continue modeling the Security Traceability matrices, please create the new ones.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272733646 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02530: (2026x) Changes of element metaclass and type

- page_id: `272733646`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272733646/2026x+Changes+of+element+metaclass+and+type
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:42:40.111+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling > (2026x) Migration User Guides > (2026x) UAF projects migration manual > (2026x) Migration from UAF 1.0 to UAF 1.1
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The following table describes element metaclasses mapping between UAF 1.0 and UAF 1.1.

| Element | UAF 1.0 [Metaclass] | UAF 1.1 [Metaclass] |
| --- | --- | --- |
| AssetRole | Element | Property |
| Desirer | Class | Element |

###### Changes types of Elements

The following table describes element metaclasses mapping between UAF 1.0 and UAF 1.1.

| Element/Property | UAF 1.0 [Type] | UAF 1.1 [Type] |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Activity::activityPerformableUnderCondition | Condition | ActualCondition |
| Measurement::environmentalContext | Condition | ActualCondition |
| LocationHolder::requiredEnvironment | Environment | ActualEnvironment |
| CapabilityConfiguration::doctrine | Constraint | StandardOperationalActivity |

For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Mapping solutions and issues' space=''].

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272734120 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02532: (2026x) Changing a path style

- page_id: `272734120`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272734120/2026x+Changing+a+path+style
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:43:19.538+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Diagramming > (2026x) Working with paths and relationships
- labels: ['path-style', 'rectilinear', 'oblique', 'bezier', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To change a path style, do either

- Right-click a path(s) on the diagram pane and select Reset Labels Positions or Remove Break Points .
- Select a path(s) on the diagram pane and on the diagram toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='remove_break_points.png'] , [ATTACHMENT filename='reset_labels_positions.png'] , or [IMAGE alt='' src=''] .
- Select a path(s) on the diagram pane and on the Edit menu click Paths , and then choose one of the following:
  - Path Style to select one of the path styles - rectilinear, oblique, or bezier.
  - Change Path Style (shortcut keys Ctrl+L) to change the path style to rectilinear, oblique, or bezier.
  - Reset Labels Positions to reset the changed path labels position to default.
  - Remove Break Points to remove all angles of the path.
- Right-click a path(s) on the diagram pane and select Symbol Properties . In the Symbol Properties dialog, change the Path Style property value by selecting rectilinear, oblique, or bezier.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To change a path style, do either</p><hr /><ul><li>Right-click a path(s) on the diagram pane and select <strong>Reset Labels Positions</strong> or <strong>Remove Break Points</strong>.</li><li>Select a path(s) on the diagram pane and on the diagram toolbar, click <ac:image ac:title="Remove Break Points" ac:alt="Remove Break Points"><ri:attachment ri:filename="remove_break_points.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>, <ac:image ac:title="Reset Labels Positions" ac:alt="Reset Labels Positions"><ri:attachment ri:filename="reset_labels_positions.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>, or<strong> <ac:image ac:title="Rectilinear" ac:alt="Rectilinear"><ri:attachment ri:filename="rectilinear.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></strong>.</li><li>Select a path(s) on the diagram pane and on the <strong>Edit</strong> menu click <strong>Paths</strong>, and then choose one of the following:<br /><ul><li><strong>Path Style</strong> to select one of the path styles - rectilinear, oblique, or bezier.</li><li><strong>Change Path Style</strong> (shortcut keys Ctrl+L) to change the path style to rectilinear, oblique, or bezier.</li><li><strong>Reset Labels Positions </strong>to reset the changed path labels position to default.</li><li><strong>Remove Break Points</strong> to remove all angles of the path.</li></ul></li><li>Right-click a path(s) on the diagram pane and select <strong>Symbol Properties</strong>. In the <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog, change the <strong>Path Style</strong> property value by selecting rectilinear, oblique, or bezier.</li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272739882 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02533: (2026x) Changing between UAF and UPDM 2 plugins

- page_id: `272739882`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272739882/2026x+Changing+between+UAF+and+UPDM+2+plugins
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:52:09.987+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) UAF 1.3 modeling
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

##### Before changing the plugin

Before changing from one plugin to another, please do the following:

1. your projects. We recommend that you back up your projects in case you need to return to your previously used plugin version (UPDM2 or UAF).
2. all [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Configuration files' space=''] in your configuration directory. We recommend making a backup if you intend to switch back.
3. You will need both UPDM 2 and UAF licenses to change between plugins. You will get both licenses during the purchase process.

- If the UAF plugin is installed, the UPDM 2 projects need to be migrated to UAF. We recommend that you make a backup of the project before the migration. Do not forget to migrate used projects before migrating the main project.
- You will not be able to migrate a UAF project back to UPDM 2.
- You will not be able to open UAF projects if the UPDM 2 plugin is installed (only applies to MagicDraw and Magic Software Architect).
- You can install only one plugin in one MagicDraw (Magic Software Architect) modeling tool instance (choose UAF 1.3 or UPDM2).

To learn which plugin is installed/used, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Using UAF and UPDM2 plugins in Cameo Enterprise Architecture' space='']

##### Changing between the UAF and UPDM 2 plugins for MagicDraw + plugins users

If you are using MagicDraw with plugins, you will need to install the new plugin via the **Resource/Plugin Manager**.

- For information about how to install the plugin, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing plugins' space='IL'] .
- Please note that only one plugin (UAF 1.3 or UPDM 2) can be installed.

##### Switching between the UAF and UPDM 2 plugins for Cameo Enterprise Architecture users

If you are working with Cameo Enterprise Architecture, the UAF plugin is already installed and the UPDM 2 installation file comes together with the Cameo Enterprise Architecture installation files. You will be able to switch between the UAF and UPDM 2 plugins.

To switch between plugins

1. In the modeling tool's main menu, select Help > Manage UPDM 2 / UAF Plugins .
2. Select the plugin you need.
3. Restart the modeling tool.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### What happens after changing between plugins

When you change the plugin:

1. The projects are not migrated automatically.
2. The configuration files are overwritten. If you have customized the diagram descriptors, perspectives, etc., we recommend making a backup if you intend to switch back.
3. The plugin is overwritten (with profiles, templates, etc.) in case of installation. We recommend that you back up your projects in case you need to return to your previously used plugin version (UPDM2 or UAF).

##### backupHow to back up your configuration files and projects

To back up the configuration files

1. In the modeling tool's main menu, click Help > About
2. In the About dialog, click Environment .
3. In the **Environment** tab, click a link near the Configuration Files . The window with your configuration files opens.
4. Select the folder of your current version and make a copy of it (add to the archive, for example, .zip).
5. Place the copied files in a safe location.

To back up the local projects

1. Gather all the projects you want to back up in one folder.
2. Make a copy of that folder or add it to an archive (for example, .zip).
3. Place the copied files in a safe location.

##### How to restore from the backup

If you need to switch back to working with your previously used plugin (UAF or UPDM2), you can do so by restoring the backups you made before switching. You will need to restore configuration files as well as projects.

To restore the configuration files from backup

1. Close the modeling tool.
2. Extract the archived configuration files.
3. Replace the current configuration files with the extracted ones.

##### Installing two modeling tools on one machine

In case you need to work with UPDM 2 and UAF projects on the same machine, you will need to have two modeling tool installations. One of them should have UPDM 2 Plugin and the second - UAF Plugin installed.

When working with two or more installations of the same modeling toolversion, you must have individual configuration files directory for each installation (by default, all the installations of the same modeling tool version use a single directory, for example, *<...>\.cameoea\19.0*).

In such case, the initial installation of a certain modeling tool version can keep on using the default configuration files directory, that is, *<...>\.cameoea\<version number>*, but you must choose a different configuration files location for each subsequent installation (see the following section).

##### customStoring configuration files in a custom location

#### NOTE: Note

Note

If you have defined to store files in the modeling tool installation directory, files will not be stored in the defined location.

To store modeling tool configuration files in a custom location

Do either:

1. Open the <modeling tool name>.properties file, which is located in <modeling tool installation directory>\bin .
2. In the JAVA_ARGS line, add the following property: -Dlocalconfig.location=<absolute path to a custom location> Sample property value-Dlocalconfig.location\=E\:\\temp\\innerPack

Or:

1. Go to the directory, where [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Configuration files' space=''] .
2. Create a new file named *redirected.location*.
3. In the file, type the absolute path to the directory, wherein modeling configuration files will be saved, e.g., C:/<directory name>.

The path in both options (*localconfig.location* property/*redirected.location* file) can contain system/environment/application related variables.

| Context of the variable | Format of the variable | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Java system property | <sys:variableName> | <sys:user.name> |
| OS environment variable | <env:variableName> | <env:USERNAME> |
| Application related variable | <app:variableName> | <app:version> |

There are only two application related variables:

| Application related variable | Description | Value example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| version | Full version of the application | 2024x Refresh3 |
| default.config.dir.name | Default modeling tool configuration directory name (.<modeling tool name>) | .magicdraw (see ) |

#### TIP: Sample path with variables

Sample path with variables

```text
<env:APPDATA>/configurations/<app:default.config.dir.name>/<sys:user.name>/<app:version>
```

##### The installation workflow

To install two modeling tools with different plugin versions

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing modeling tools' space='IL'] the first modeling tool.
2. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Configuration files' space=''] for the modeling tool configuration files (create a new folder for that).
3. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing modeling tools' space='IL'] the second modeling tool to a different location.
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Configuration files' space=''] for the modeling tool configuration files (create another folder for that).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Before changing the plugin</h3><p>Before changing from one plugin to another, please do the following:</p><ol><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:link ac:anchor="backup"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Back up]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> your projects.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d9c01013-78d2-45ec-82d4-e4cf8e70ff54"><ac:rich-text-body><p>We recommend that you back up your projects in case you need to return to your previously used plugin version (UPDM2 or UAF).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="backup"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Back up]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> all <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Configuration files" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[configuration files]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> in your configuration directory. <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">We recommend making a backup if you intend to switch back.<br /></span></li><li><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">You will need both UPDM 2 and UAF licenses to change between plugins. You will need to restore configuration files as well as projects.</p><p><br /></p><p>To restore the configuration files from backup</p><hr /><ol><li>Close the modeling tool.</li><li>Extract the archived configuration files.</li><li>Replace the current configuration files with the extracted ones.</li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><h3>Installing two modeling tools on one machine</h3><p>In case you need to work with UPDM 2 and UAF projects on the same machine, you will need to have two modeling tool installations. One of them should have UPDM 2 Plugin and the second - UAF Plugin installed.</p><p>When working with two or more installations of the same <span style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-red-bolder,#c9372c);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">modeling tool</span> </span>version, you must have individual configuration files directory for each installation (by default, all the installations of the same <span style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-red-bolder,#c9372c);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">modeling tool</span> </span> version use a single directory, for example, <em>&lt;...&gt;\.cameoea\19.0</em>).</p><p>In such case, the initial installation of a certain <span style="color:var(--ds-background-accent-red-bolder,#c9372c);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">modeling tool</span> </span> version can keep on using the default configuration files directory, that is, <em>&lt;...&gt;\.cameoea\&lt;version number&gt;</em>, but you must choose a different configuration files location for each subsequent installation (see the following section).</p><h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8f1fed61-4306-4eff-abdd-4fe871dedb79"><ac:parameter ac:name="">custom</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>Storing configuration files in a custom location<br /><br /></h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a9e64a11-d251-4e2c-a18f-a287f0631189"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Note</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you have defined to store files in the modeling tool installation directory, files will not be stored in the defined location.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>To store modeling tool configuration files in a custom location</p><hr /><p>Do either:</p><ol><li>Open the <em>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;.properties</em> file, which is located in <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\bin</em>.</li><li>In the JAVA_ARGS line, add the following property:<br /><pre> -Dlocalconfig.location=&lt;absolute path to a custom location&gt;<br /><br /></pre><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c3647347-253c-4233-adbd-db8af1c6d06b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Sample property value</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>-Dlocalconfig.location\=E\:\\temp\\innerPack</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br />Or:</p><ol><li>Go to the directory, where <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Configuration files" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[the configuration files are stored]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><a>.</a></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Create a new file named <em>redirected.location</em>.</p></li><li>In the file, type the absolute path to the directory, wherein modeling configuration files will be saved, e.g., C:/&lt;directory name&gt;.</li></ol><p><br /></p><p>The path in both options (<em>localconfig.location</em> property/<em>redirected.location</em> file) can contain system/environment/application related variables. </p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Context of the variable</th><th>Format of the variable</th><th>Example</th></tr><tr><td>Java system property</td><td>&lt;sys:variableName&gt;</td><td>&lt;sys:user.name&gt;</td></tr><tr><td>OS environment variable</td><td>&lt;env:variableName&gt;</td><td>&lt;env:USERNAME&gt;</td></tr><tr><td>Application related variable</td><td>&lt;app:variableName&gt;</td><td>&lt;app:version&gt;</td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p>There are only two application related variables:</p><table class="wrapped"><colgroup><col /><col /><col /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Application related variable</th><th>Description</th><th>Value example</th></tr><tr><td>version</td><td>Full version of the application</td><td>2024x Refresh3</td></tr><tr><td>default.config.dir.name</td><td>Default modeling tool configuration directory name (.<span>&lt;modeling tool name&gt;</span>)</td><td>.magicdraw (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Configuration files" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Modeling tool file naming]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>)</td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b0a1eb04-9765-4a56-87b3-4ff3ac2a34fa"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Sample path with variables</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><pre>&lt;env:APPDATA&gt;/configurations/&lt;app:default.config.dir.name&gt;/&lt;sys:user.name&gt;/&lt;app:version&gt;</pre></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>The installation workflow </h3><p>To install two modeling tools with different plugin versions</p><hr /><ol><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="IL" ri:content-title="Installing modeling tools" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Install]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> the first modeling tool.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Configuration files" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Change the predefined location]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for the modeling tool configuration files (create a new folder for that).</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="IL" ri:content-title="Installing modeling tools" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Install ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>the second modeling tool to a different location.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Configuration files" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Change the predefined location]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for the modeling tool configuration files (create another folder for that).</li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272732906 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02534: (2026x) Changing diagram type

- page_id: `272732906`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272732906/2026x+Changing+diagram+type
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:41:25.939+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Diagramming
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The diagram type can be changed to another type of diagram if both diagram types are compatible.

#### NOTE: Diagram Elements

Diagram Elements

Diagram elements are not converted.

###### **Uses for changing the diagram type:**

- To migrate with the existing project to a diagram type that was not available until then. For example, migrating a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Class diagram' space=''] to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SysML Block Definition Diagram' space=''] .
- To migrate with the existing project from the diagram type, which will be erased from your project. For example, if the user has decided to erase a plugin, they would need to convert that plugin's specific diagram to standard diagrams.

###### **Diagram conversion scenarios:**

- Any static diagram can be converted to another static diagram.
- Any dynamic diagram can be converted to another diagram if and only if both diagrams are based on the same diagram type and both diagrams are compatible.

To change the diagram type

1. Select one or more of the same type diagrams in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Model Browser' space=''] (Containment, Structure, or Diagram tree).
2. From the shortcut menu, choose Refactor > Change Type To . In the open dialog, select the desired diagram type. [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2017-06-06 at 5.02.07 PM.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target">The diagram type can be changed to another type of diagram if both diagram types are compatible.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d10c128c-e709-43cf-b861-e3c7eba7699c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Diagram Elements</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Diagram elements are not converted.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><h4><strong>Uses for changing the diagram type:</strong></h4><ul><li>To migrate with the existing project to a diagram type that was not available until then. For example, migrating a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Class diagram" /></ac:link> to the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SysML Block Definition Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[SysML v1 Block Definition diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li><li>To migrate with the existing project from the diagram type, which will be erased from your project. For example, if the user has decided to erase a plugin, they would need to convert that plugin's specific diagram to standard diagrams. </li></ul><p><br /></p><h4><strong>Diagram conversion scenarios:</strong></h4><ul><li>Any static diagram can be converted to another static diagram. </li><li>Any dynamic diagram can be converted to another diagram if and only if both diagrams are based on the same diagram type and both diagrams are compatible.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p>To change the diagram type</p><hr /><ol><li>Select one or more of the same type diagrams in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Model Browser" /></ac:link> (Containment, Structure, or Diagram tree).</li><li>From the shortcut menu, choose <strong>Refactor </strong>&gt;<strong> Change Type To</strong>. In the open dialog, select the desired diagram type.<br /><ac:image ac:height="400"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Screen Shot 2017-06-06 at 5.02.07 PM.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Changing diagram type" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /></p><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272737934 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02535: (2026x) Changing interface style

- page_id: `272737934`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272737934/2026x+Changing+interface+style
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:49:24.452+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Getting started > (2026x) Understanding the user interface
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Look and Feel Themes

1426180851

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Look and Feel Themes

1426180854

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Look and Feel Themes

1426180852

#### EXCERPT: Look and Feel Themes

INLINE

You can change the appearance of your modeling tool windows, dialogs, and menus. The **Look and Feel** menu allows you to personalize the user interface using a number of predefined styles and themes. You can also create a custom theme with your favorite colors and fonts.

To change the interface style

1. In the main menu of a modeling tool, select Options > Look and Feel .
2. Select the desired style from the list of available styles.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Look and Feel menu options.

#### NOTE: Important

Important

- The selected style may not look exactly like the style in related operating systems.
- Depending on your operating system, some style options may be unavailable.

##### Creating a custom theme

Define your own interface style by creating a custom theme. The **Custom** theme option is located in the **Look and Feel Themes** submenu which also offers 8 predefined themes. The **Custom** theme allows you to specify text fonts and sizes of the desired interface elements as well as their colors.

To create a custom theme

1. In the main menu of a modeling tool, select Options > Look and Feel > Metal .
2. In the same menu, select Options > Look and Feel > Look and Feel Themes > Custom .
3. In the open dialog, change the values of the desired style properties.
4. Click OK .

#### NOTE: Look and Feel Themes

Look and Feel Themes

The themes listed in the **Look and Feel Themes** submenu are only available if you select the **Metal** style.

1426180850

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Understanding the user interface' space='']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Model Browser' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Image Library tab' space='']

The <strong>Custom</strong> theme option is located in the <strong>Look and Feel Themes</strong> submenu which also offers 8 predefined themes. The <strong>Custom</strong> theme allows you to specify text fonts and sizes of the desired interface elements as well as their colors.<br /><br /></p><p>To create a custom theme</p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu of a modeling tool, select <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Look and Feel</strong> &gt;<strong> Metal</strong>.</li><li>In the same menu, select <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Look and Feel</strong> &gt;<strong> Look and Feel Themes</strong> &gt;<strong> Custom</strong>.</li><li>In the open dialog, change the values of the desired style properties.</li><li>Click <strong>OK</strong>.</li></ol><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2c726df5-edb3-47c1-b619-b75abdc6764b"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Look and Feel Themes</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>The themes listed in the <strong>Look and Feel Themes</strong> submenu are only available if you select the <strong>Metal</strong> style.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="00bc8004-9c35-455b-83a0-3ce093c77b92"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1426180850</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Understanding the user interface" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Main menu" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Toolbars" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Model Browser" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Image Library tab" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272729435 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02536: (2026x) Changing the context

- page_id: `272729435`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272729435/2026x+Changing+the+context
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:35:18.277+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Diagrams > (2026x) Analysis diagrams > (2026x) Relation Map
- labels: ['relation-map-change-context', 'relation-map-context', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Context is the element, from which a relation map is drawn and the analysis is started. You can set any element as the context.

To set an element as the context of the relation map

- Drag the selected element from the Model Browser to the relation map.
- In the Criteria area, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='select_context_element_button.png'] button next to the Context box and then select the element in the Select Context Element dialog.
- If the **Make Element as Context on Selection** option is turned on, click any node to make it the new context of the structure. To turn this option on, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] on the toolbar and select **Make Element as Context on Selection**.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Context is the element, from which a relation map is drawn and the analysis is started. You can set any element as the context.</p><p>To set an element as the context of the relation map</p><hr /><ul><li>Drag the selected element from the Model Browser to the relation map.</li><li>In the Criteria area, click the <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="21"><ri:attachment ri:filename="select_context_element_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_MD buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> button next to the Context box and then select the element in the <strong>Select Context Element</strong><span> </span>dialog.<span> </span></li><li><p>If the <strong>Make Element as Context on Selection</strong> option is turned on, click any node to make it the new context of the structure. To turn this option on, click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="options_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> on the toolbar and select <strong>Make Element as Context on Selection</strong>.</p></li></ul><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272732885 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02537: (2026x) Changing the label display mode

- page_id: `272732885`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272732885/2026x+Changing+the+label+display+mode
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:41:23.800+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x) Modeling structure with Blocks > (2026x) Connecting Blocks in SysML Internal Block Diagram > (2026x) Implied Connectors
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The label of implied relation provides useful information about actual ports and Connectors. You can define how much of the information should be shown in the label by changing the **Label Display Mode**in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Symbol Properties dialog' space='']**Symbol Properties** dialog.The procedure below describes how to change the label display mode and explains the meaning each of display mode.

To change the label display mode

1. Right-click the implied relation.
2. Select **Symbol Properties**.
3. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Symbol Properties dialog' space='']**Symbol Properties** dialog, find the **Label Display Mode** property and select one of the following value:
  - 
    - **Do Not Display** - to hide the label.
    - **Compact**(default) - to show a short summary in the label of how many actual connectors are underneath.
    - **Detailed** - to show all names or types of ports on both actual connector ends.
4. Click **OK**.

##### Compact Label Display Mode

It allows you to show a short summary in the label of how many actual connectors are underneath. It also displays names of the first level ports at both connector ends, if actual connectors exist only between two ports. Otherwise, the names will not be shown. The figure below demonstrates the situation, when all labels of implied relations provide a different information despite the same **Compact** display mode is selected for all of them.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Three labels of the implied relation with Compact display mode which provides different information.

1. The label of the implied relation between Part Property typed by *TV* Block and diagram frame indicates that only 1 actual connector exists between part and diagram frame because the number is not shown in brackets. The *power in* and socket 2 names in the label shows the names of the first level ports. 
2. The label of the implied relation between Part Properties typed by *TV* and *TV Tuner* Blocks indicates that 18 actual connectors exist between those parts. The *hdmi*name in the label shows the names of the first level ports. 
3. The label of the implied relation between Part Property typed by *TV Tuner* Block and diagram frame indicates that 9 actual connectors exist between part and diagram frame. The names of ports are not displayed in the label because actual connectors (which are underneath of implied relation) have different first level ports at both ends.

##### Detailed Label Display Mode

It allows you to show all names or types of ports on both actual connector ends. If actual Connectors are between two ports, the label is the same as**Compact** mode.The figure below demonstrates the situation, when all labels of implied relations provide a different information despite the same **Detailed** display mode is selected for all of them.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Three labels of the implied relation with Detailed display mode which provides different information.

In the figure above, the labels numbered 1. and 2. in the illustration, provide the same information as **Compact** display mode because actual connectors exist only between two ports. The label between the *TV Tuner* Block and diagram frame (3.) shows that 8 connectors exist between*internet* and *LAN socket* ports and 1 connector between *power in* and *socket 2* ports. It is useful to set the**Detailed**mode when you want to see all first level port names in the implied relation label (see figure below).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The implied relation with the Detailed label display mode.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SysML Internal Block Diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Connecting parts through interface' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Hiding direction arrow on port shape' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying parts and ports in SysML v1' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Autowire Parts' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Property path notation' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Enforce Ports Compatibility mode' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Displaying elements' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Proxy Port' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Flow Port' space='MED']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Full Port' space='MED']

**Sample model**

The sample model used in the figures on this page is the **Implied Connectors**sample model. Download [ATTACHMENT filename='Implied Connectors.mdzip'].

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">The label of implied relation provides useful information about actual ports and Connectors. You can define how much of the information should be shown in the label by changing <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">the <strong>Label Display Mode </strong>in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</span><span class="confluence-link"> </span>The procedure below describes how to change the label display mode and explains the meaning each of display mode.</span></p><p><br /></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">To change the label display mode</span></p><hr /><ol><li data-uuid="140d6462-a2a2-495a-b5c2-696dfd96e519"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Right-click the implied relation.</span></li><li data-uuid="650e6d7e-307d-4e51-b48e-26661c12f79d"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Select <strong>Symbol Properties</strong>.<br /></span></li><li data-uuid="a92a275a-1779-4653-933f-2926b47f637e"><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>, f</span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">ind the <strong>Label Display Mode</strong> property and select one of the following value:</span></p><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;"><ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><strong>Do Not Display</strong> - to hide the label.</span></p></li><li><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><strong>Compact </strong>(default) - to show a short summary in the label of how many actual connectors are underneath.<br /></span></p></li><li><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><strong>Detailed</strong> - to show all names or types of ports on both actual connector ends.</span></p></li></ul></li></ul></li><li data-uuid="26827236-af20-4d46-b521-862bebf9979b"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Click <strong>OK</strong>.</span></li></ol><h3><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Compact Label Display Mode</span></h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">It allows you to show a short summary in the label of how many actual connectors are underneath. It also displays names of the first level ports at both connector ends, if actual connectors exist only between two ports. Otherwise, the names will not be shown.</span> The figure below demonstrates the situation, when all labels of implied relations provide a different information despite the same <strong>Compact</strong> display mode is selected for all of them.</p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="compact_label_display_mode.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Changing the label display mode" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Three labels of the implied relation with Compact display mode which provides different information.</h6><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><p>1. The label of the implied relation between Part Property typed by <em>TV</em> Block and diagram frame indicates that only 1 actual connector exists between part and diagram frame because the number is not shown in brackets. The <em>power in</em> and socket 2 names in the label shows the names of the first level ports.<br />2. The label of the implied relation between Part Properties typed by <em>TV</em> and <em>TV Tuner</em> Blocks indicates that 18 actual connectors exist between those parts. The <em>hdmi </em>name in the label shows the names of the first level ports.<br />3. The label of the implied relation between Part Property typed by <em>TV Tuner</em> Block and diagram frame indicates that 9 actual connectors exist between part and diagram frame. The names of ports are not displayed in the label because actual connectors (which are underneath of implied relation) have different first level ports at both ends.</p><h3><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">Detailed Label Display Mode </span></h3><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">It allows you to show all names or types of ports on both actual connector ends. If actual Connectors are between two ports, the label is the same as<strong> Compact</strong> mode.<span style="color:var(--ds-text,#000000);"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"> The figure below demonstrates the situation, when all labels of implied relations provide a different information despite the same <strong>Detailed</strong> display mode is selected for all of them.</span><br /></span></span></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="detailed_label_display_mode.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Changing the label display mode" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></span></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Three labels of the implied relation with Detailed display mode which provides different information.</h6><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#333333);">In the figure above, the labels numbered 1. and 2. in the illustration, provide the same information as <strong>Compact</strong> display mode because actual connectors exist only between two ports. The label between the <em>TV Tuner</em> Block and diagram frame (3.) shows that 8 connectors exist between<em> internet</em> and <em>LAN socket</em> ports and 1 connector between <em>power in</em> and <em>socket 2</em> ports. It is useful to set the<strong> Detailed </strong>mode when you want to see all first level port names in the implied relation label (see figure below).<br /></span></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Detailed label mode.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Changing the label display mode" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The implied relation with the Detailed label display mode.</h6></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_equal"><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li class="with-breadcrumbs"
