**On this page**

3

492179711

##### Object, Boundary, Entity, and Control

All Object, Boundary, Entity, Control elements in EA are [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Instance Specification' space='MED']. After conversion, their UML element types will remain, and a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Lifeline' space='MED']and an OwnedAttribute element will be created to represent each of them in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Communication diagram' space=''] in which they occur.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Object, Boundary, Entity, and Control (diagram view).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Object, Boundary, Entity, and Control (Containment tree).

Other elements that can be drawn in a Communication diagram will be handled in a similar manner.

##### Exception Elements

Some of the elements that can be drawn in a Communication diagram using EA are not supported by MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM. Consequently, their displaying parts will not be imported. These elements include [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='MED'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Activity diagram' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Action' space='MED'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Diagram frame' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State' space='MED'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction' space='MED'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exception Handler' space='MED'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Object Node' space='MED'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing regions' space='MED'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Object Node' space='MED'], Trigger, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Expansion Region and Expansion Nodes' space='MED'], and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction' space='MED'].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Exception elements.

###### Realization and Nesting

The Realization element type in EA is *uml:Realization*. The Nesting element type in EA is exported in XMI as *uml:Dependency*. The Realization and Nesting lines in EA are not supported in the Communication diagram; therefore, only their model data will be imported, not the displaying parts.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Realization and Nesting.

##### Association

Every Association relationship drawn in a Communication diagram in EA will have a Connector line created for each of them. The elements attached to both ends of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association End' space='MED']line will have a Lifeline element created to represent each of them. The Association lines and the elements attached to them will not be removed. However, the elements that will be shown in the diagram frame will be the Lifeline elements and the Connector lines created to represent them.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Association.

If an Association line is connected to the InstanceSpecification elements, its data will be removed. If an Association line is linked between two InstanceSpecification elements, it will be transformed into an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Instance Specification' space='MED']element. This is one of the constraints belonging to the Communication diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Association Line between InstanceSpecifications.

##### Message

Messages can be created on Connectors and will be imported to MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Message.

##### Operations

An InstanceSpecification cannot contain Operation elements. If the XMI file from EA has some InstanceSpecification elements containing Operation elements, those Operations will be removed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Operations.

492185720

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Communication diagram' space='']

## PAGE 02561: (2026x) Comparing projects

- page_id: `272730398`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272730398/2026x+Comparing+projects
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:37:11.467+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Model analysis
- labels: ['base', 'compared', 'compare-concepts', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

641794038

641794050

641794040

The Compare Projects functionality allows you to compare:

- Two server project versions
- Open server project with a local or server project version
- Open a local project with a server project version

Various different properties of the elements that share the same ID in different projects are compared.

##### Basic concepts

For a better understanding of the further material, learn the following concepts.

| Concept | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Base | The original project version without changes. |
| Compared | A project version that contains modifications added to the original project version. |

641794035

**Related pages**

2

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272732996 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02562: (2026x) Compartments

- page_id: `272732996`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272732996/2026x+Compartments
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:41:40.992+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Diagramming
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The compartment is an area on the shape or next to the shape that is dedicated to the specific type of information.

There are the following types of compartments:

- Compartment boxes. For example, the class shape has compartments that put attributes, operations, and signals into boxes. [ATTACHMENT filename='compartments.png']

- You can hide compartments by suppressing them or expand the hidden compartments to see their data.
- Compartment names are displayed by default. However, you can hide them by changing their visibility settings either in the Symbol Properties dialog or the Project Options dialog. Each compartment has its own property for visibility settings (for example, Show Attributes Compartment Name ).

- Compartments that are displayed next to the element name on the shape. For example, a shape has compartments for stereotypes, constraints, and others.

- Stereotypes (or stereotypes compartment) are displayed above the element name.
- Constraints (or constraints compartment) are displayed under the element name.
- By default, tagged values are displayed under the element name, or you can select to display tagged values in the compartment box.
- Element Properties are displayed in the compartment box.

- Compartments that are displayed next to the element name, next to the shape. For example, an Actor has a compartment for element properties, a compartment for stereotypes, behaviors, and others.
- Compartments on paths that are displayed next to the path label. For example, a compartment for element properties, a compartment for stereotypes, and others.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Diagramming' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Path compartments' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272738990 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 02563: (2026x) Compensation

- page_id: `272738990`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738990/2026x+Compensation
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-11-25T14:45:33.040+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) BPMN modeling > (2026x) Business Process Model and Notation Concepts > (2026x) BPMN Process Diagram
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1486067289

1486067289

1486067289

**Description**

A Compensation in business process modeling is concerned with undoing steps that have already been successfully completed because their results and possible side effects are no longer desired and need to be reversed. If an Activity is still active, it cannot be compensated and needs to be canceled. The cancellation of a SubProcess can produce a compensation of the already successfully completed portions of an active Activity.

A Compensation is performed by a compensation handler. A compensation handler performs the steps necessary to reverse the effects of an Activity. For a SubProcess, the compensation handler will have access to the SubProcess data once they have been completed (“snapshot data”).

A compensation handler is a set of Activities that is not connected to other portions of the BPMN model. The compensation handler starts with either of the Compensation Events:

- Compensation Boundary Event
- The handler’s Start Event (in case of a Compensation Event SubProcess)

A compensation handler connected through a boundary event can only perform a “black-box” compensation of the original Activity. This compensation is modeled with a specialized Compensation Activity, which is connected to the boundary event through an association. The Compensation Activity, which can be either a Task or a SubProcess, is marked to show that it is used for compensation only and is located outside the normal flow of the Process.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Compensation handler connected through Compensation Boundary Event

Another way to model a compensation is using a compensation handler, which starts with a Start Event of an Event SubProcess that is contained within a Process or SubProcess. Just like any other Compensation Activities, a Compensation Event SubProcess is located outside the normal flow of a process. The Event SubProcess, which is marked with a dotted line boundary, has access to data that are part of the parent, which is a snapshot at the point in time when the parent has been completed. A Compensation Event SubProcess can recursively trigger a compensation for activities contained in its parent.

**Example**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Compensation defined by Event SubProcess

1486067288

**Related elements**

- BPMN Process
- SubProcesses
- Compensation Start Event
- Escalation Throwing Intermediate Event
- Compensation Boundary Event
- Association

**Related diagrams**

- BPMN Process Diagram
- BPMN Collaboration Diagram

**Related procedure**

- Using Activities

The cancellation of a SubProcess can produce a compensation of the already successfully completed portions of an active Activity.</p><p>A Compensation is performed by a compensation handler. A compensation handler performs the steps necessary to reverse the effects of an Activity. For a SubProcess, the compensation handler will have access to the SubProcess data once they have been completed (“snapshot data”).</p><p>A compensation handler is a set of Activities that is not connected to other portions of the BPMN model. The compensation handler starts with either of the Compensation Events:</p><ul><li>Compensation Boundary Event </li><li>The handler’s Start Event (in case of a Compensation Event SubProcess)</li></ul><p>A compensation handler connected through a boundary event can only perform a “black-box” compensation of the original Activity. This compensation is modeled with a specialized Compensation Activity, which is connected to the boundary event through an association. The Compensation Activity, which can be either a Task or a SubProcess, is marked to show that it is used for compensation only and is located outside the normal flow of the Process.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="compensation_handler_I.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Compensation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>Compensation handler connected through Compensation Boundary Event</h6><p>Another way to model a compensation is using a compensation handler, which starts with a Start Event of an Event SubProcess that is contained within a Process or SubProcess. Just like any other Compensation Activities, a Compensation Event SubProcess is located outside the normal flow of a process. The Event SubProcess, which is marked with a dotted line boundary, has access to data that are part of the parent, which is a snapshot at the point in time when the parent has been completed. A Compensation Event SubProcess can recursively trigger a compensation for activities contained in its parent.</p><p><strong>Example</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="compensation_handler_II.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Compensation" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>Compensation defined by Event SubProcess</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e9ae814f-47ab-4002-9719-0dc6a5abbc53"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1486067288</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) BPMN+Process">BPMN Process</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) SubProcesses">SubProcesses</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Compensation+Start+Event">Compensation Start Event</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Escalation+Throwing+Intermediate+Event">Escalation Throwing Intermediate Event</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Compensation+Boundary+Event">Compensation Boundary Event</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Association">Association</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related diagrams</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link parent-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) BPMN+Process+Diagram">BPMN Process Diagram</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) BPMN+Collaboration+Diagram">BPMN Collaboration Diagram</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedure</strong></p><ul><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Using+Activities">Using Activities</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272738379 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 02564: (2026x) Compensation Boundary Event

- page_id: `272738379`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738379/2026x+Compensation+Boundary+Event
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-11-25T14:45:04.897+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) BPMN modeling > (2026x) Business Process Model and Notation Concepts > (2026x) BPMN Process Diagram > (2026x) Boundary Events
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

A Compensation Boundary Event is used to catch a Compensation Event. The event will be triggered by a compensation event. When the event is triggered, a Compensation Activity, which is associated with it, will be performed.

The Compensation Boundary Event in this sense does not affect the interrupting or non-interrupting aspect. Compensations can only be triggered after the completion of an Activity to which they are attached. Thus, they cannot interrupt the Activity. The boundary of the event is always solid.

**Notation**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Related elements**

- Boundary Events
- Activities
- Compensation

**Related diagrams**

- BPMN Process Diagram
- BPMN Collaboration Diagram
- BPMN Choreography Diagram

**Related procedure**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating and Using a BPMN Event' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>A Compensation Boundary Event is used to catch a Compensation Event. The event will be triggered by a compensation event. When the event is triggered, a Compensation Activity, which is associated with it, will be performed.</p><p>The Compensation Boundary Event in this sense does not affect the interrupting or non-interrupting aspect. Compensations can only be triggered after the completion of an Activity to which they are attached. Thus, they cannot interrupt the Activity. The boundary of the event is always solid.</p><p><strong>Notation</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="boundary_event_compensation.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Compensation Boundary Event" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link parent-link" data-uuid="a84bb536-9bc2-4eda-8460-1df1e60aacaa"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Boundary+Events">Boundary Events</a></li><li data-uuid="d44b66c1-b59d-4f5d-b143-e6eeeadae6a9"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Activities">Activities</a></li><li data-uuid="f0ad2bc5-b639-42d3-a1b9-ca25b25be7e6"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Compensation">Compensation</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related diagrams</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link" data-uuid="b7c7721b-74c8-48a4-bcda-9e37fbc865ca"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) BPMN+Process+Diagram">BPMN Process Diagram</a></li><li data-uuid="6ad61fde-f4b3-4faf-9fec-6372f0ed179a"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) BPMN+Collaboration+Diagram">BPMN Collaboration Diagram</a></li><li data-uuid="0db50742-9df7-47ad-adef-c94fc0f3bc9d"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) BPMN+Choreography+Diagram">BPMN Choreography Diagram</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedure</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="ffd34258-fb27-4dd6-863a-333ba832b0b9"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating and Using a BPMN Event" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating and Using an Event]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272738413 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 02565: (2026x) Compensation End Event

- page_id: `272738413`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738413/2026x+Compensation+End+Event
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-11-25T14:45:14.386+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) BPMN modeling > (2026x) Business Process Model and Notation Concepts > (2026x) BPMN Process Diagram > (2026x) End Events
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

A Compensation End Event indicates that a compensation is necessary.

- If an Activity, which has successfully been completed, is identified, that Activity will be compensated.
- If no Activity is identified, all successfully completed Activities visible from the Compensation End Event will be compensated in reverse order of their sequence flows.

To be compensated, an Activity must have a Compensation Boundary Event or contain a Compensation Event SubProcess.

**Notation**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Related elements**

- End Events
- Compensation

**Related diagrams**

- BPMN Process Diagram
- BPMN Collaboration Diagram

**Related procedure**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating and Using a BPMN Event' space='']

## PAGE 02567: (2026x) Compensation Throwing Intermediate Event

- page_id: `272738389`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738389/2026x+Compensation+Throwing+Intermediate+Event
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-11-25T14:44:56.336+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) BPMN modeling > (2026x) Business Process Model and Notation Concepts > (2026x) BPMN Process Diagram > (2026x) Intermediate Throwing Event
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Description**

A Compensation Throwing Intermediate Event indicates that a compensation is necessary.

If an Activity, which has been successfully completed, is identified, then it will be compensated.

If no Activity is identified, all successfully completed Activities visible from a Compensation Throwing Intermediate Event will be compensated in reverse order of their sequence flows. To be compensated, the Activity must have a Boundary Compensation Event or contain a Compensation Event SubProcess.

**Notation**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Related elements**

- Compensation
- Intermediate Throwing Event

**Related diagrams**

- BPMN Process Diagram
- BPMN Collaboration Diagram

**Related procedure**

To be compensated, the Activity must have a Boundary Compensation Event or contain a Compensation Event SubProcess.</p><p><strong>Notation</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="intermediate_throwing_event_compensation.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Compensation Throwing Intermediate Event" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="1b687733-2877-4b08-9664-4324117d68fd"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Compensation">Compensation</a></li><li class="ancestor-link parent-link" data-uuid="577722ca-c731-4561-aef7-b16cdc0c8c54"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Intermediate+Throwing+Event">Intermediate Throwing Event</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related diagrams</strong></p><ul><li class="ancestor-link" data-uuid="17aa2cb9-03de-4891-b531-74acee354cb1"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) BPMN+Process+Diagram">BPMN Process Diagram</a></li><li data-uuid="5cb66d38-5f2a-4f34-9bae-4564ae1e8844"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) BPMN+Collaboration+Diagram">BPMN Collaboration Diagram</a></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedure</strong></p><ul><li data-uuid="01a364c4-0097-4810-97dd-7f72080042f6"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating and Using a BPMN Event" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Creating and Using an Event]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272737741 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02568: (2026x) Complete diagrams

- page_id: `272737741`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272737741/2026x+Complete+diagrams
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:49:17.216+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Diagramming
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Updating multiple paths

31649184

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Updating multiple paths

31649186

31649182

**On this page**

4

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Updating multiple paths

31649185

##### Marking diagrams as complete

When you finish working on a diagram, you expect that the diagram layout will not change. However, if the content of your model is changed, the diagram layout changes as well; shapes are automatically resized to fit new content and labels are automatically displayed on paths. This can cause symbols to overlap and paths to be left hanging in a diagram, as illustrated below. So you must go back to the finished diagram and fix its layout.To save time, you can mark diagrams as complete andprevent their layout from being automatically updated.

700500

To mark a diagram as complete

- Right-click the diagram on the diagram pane or in the model browser and from the menu select Complete .

When a diagram is marked as complete, the following happens:

- The «complete» stereotype is applied to a diagram. This way you can easily indicate complete diagrams in the model browser without opening them.
- Auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels is suspended. This means that the diagram layout is not automatically changed when the model content changes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The figure displays an example of a complete diagram.

#### NOTE: Alternative

Alternative

You can suspend auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels in a diagram without marking it as complete in one of the following ways:

- To suspend auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels for individual symbols, o pen the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Symbol Properties dialog' space='']**Symbol Properties** dialog of a symbol or a group of symbols, and set the Suspend Auto-Resizing of Shapes and/or Suspend Auto-Displaying of Labels properties to true .
- To suspend auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels for all the diagram symbols, o pen the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specifying diagram style properties' space='']**Diagram Properties** dialog of a diagram, and set the Suspend Auto-Resizing of Shapes and/or Suspend Auto-Displaying of Labels properties to true.

##### Updating complete diagram layout

When a diagram is marked as complete, the symbols with suspended content related to model changes are highlighted in blue (see the figure above). You can update these symbols manually, as described below.

To update shapes

1. In a complete diagram, select the highlighted shape you want to update.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Drag one of the selection handles on the corner of the shape to resize it. The suspended content is dynamically displayed after resizing the shape.
  - Click the smart manipulator on the bottom of the shape, as shown below. [ATTACHMENT filename='updating_shape.png']
  - Click [ATTACHMENT filename='solver.png'] on the shape toolbar and select Resize to Fit Content .

#### TIP: Updating multiple shapes

Updating multiple shapes

To update multiple shapes in a complete diagram, select the highlighted shapes you want to update and click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] in the diagram toolbar.

To update paths

1. In a complete diagram, select the highlighted path you want to update.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Click the smart manipulator on the path, as shown below. [ATTACHMENT filename='updating_path.png']
  - Click [ATTACHMENT filename='solver.png'] on the path toolbar and select Show Suspended Labels .

#### TIP: Updating multiple paths

Updating multiple paths

To update multiple paths in a complete diagram, select the highlighted paths you want to update and click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] in the diagram toolbar.

##### Marking diagrams as incomplete

If you need to resume normal work on a diagram, mark it as incomplete, as described below.

To mark a diagram as incomplete

1. Right-click a diagram on the diagram pane or in the model browser and select the Complete option to clear its check box.
2. When the question dialog is displayed, do one of the following:
  - Select **Yes**, to continue suspending auto-resizing of shapes and/or auto-displaying of labels.
  - Select No , to resume auto-resizing of shapes and/or auto-displaying of labels.

If you select**No**in step 2, all diagram symbols are automatically resized to fit suspended content, andyou can continue working on the diagram as usual. If you select **Yes**, auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels in the diagram are still suspended, even though the diagram is not complete.

##### Customizing complete diagram behavior

By default, auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels in a complete diagram are suspended. Also, the symbols with suspended content due to model changes are highlighted in blue. You can change this default behavior in the **Project Options** dialog.

To customize complete diagram behavior

1. In the main menu of your modeling tool, go to Options > Project .
2. On the left side of the Project Options dialog, expand the General node and select Diagrams .
3. On the right side of the dialog, specify the desired options in the Complete Diagrams group:
  - Suspend Auto-Resizing of Shapes - set to true to keep the original size of shapes while editing the model.
  - Suspend Auto-Displaying of Labels - set to true if you do not want to automatically show labels in a diagram while editing the model.
  - Highlight Suspended Symbols - set to true to highlight symbols that have suspended content related to model changes.

When you specify project options listed in step 3, they are applied to all diagrams that are marked as complete after specification.

##### **Check complete diagrams**

To check complete diagrams

- In the main menu of your modeling tool, select Analyze > Check Complete Diagrams .

The **Check Complete Diagrams**action checks for complete diagrams without completely visible content by running the following three validation rules:

| Validation rule | Error message | Severity | Profile |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Suspended Autodisplaying | The symbol content is not completely visible. Auto-displaying of labels is suspended. | warning | UML Standard Profile::Validation Profile |
| Suspended Autoresizing | The symbol content is not completely visible. Auto-resizing of shapes is suspended. | warning | UML Standard Profile::Validation Profile |
| Suspended Autochanges | The diagram contains symbols whose content is not completely visible. | warning | UML Standard Profile::Validation Profile |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fe6478ea-38ed-4100-a010-476f2f82af3c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">31649184</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="65c27722-ae16-4182-ad68-70e6a9d58995"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">31649186</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f60a082b-0878-442a-988c-aacb5c806494"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">31649182</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1a3d3a0d-87b9-4be2-a1e6-fff6474fc74c"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">4</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e7bde8e3-a27c-4d12-bd7b-ce56f19ad8ad"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">31649185</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><h3>Marking diagrams as complete</h3><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="834fe518-37ef-4695-8dc1-9d724225be7d">When you finish working on a diagram, you expect that the diagram layout <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="8699c10a-64e7-443e-97d2-bf23bbe489f4">will not change</ac:inline-comment-marker>. However, if the content of your model is changed, the diagram layout changes as well; <span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">shapes are automatically resized to fit new content and labels are automatically displayed on paths. This can cause symbols to overlap and paths to be left hanging in a diagram, as illustrated below. So you must go back to the finished diagram and fix its layout. </span>To save time, you can mark diagrams as complete and </ac:inline-comment-marker><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="d5ee668f-8ddb-4894-b55e-7caa5b86134e"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="834fe518-37ef-4695-8dc1-9d724225be7d">prevent their layout from being automatically updated.</ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:structured-macro ac:name="widget" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="171704bf-9248-4ad2-8488-15ce80247af2"><ac:parameter ac:name="width">700</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="url"><ri:url ri:value="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=lPj4knb0JfY&amp;feature=youtu.be" /></ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="height">500</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);"><br /></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(112,112,112);"><br /></span></p><p>To mark a diagram as complete</p><hr /><ul><li>Right-click the diagram on the diagram pane or in the model browser and from the menu select <strong>Complete</strong>.</li></ul><p><br /><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="d31772ee-38ea-4cc3-b158-7937f5d04955">When a diagram is marked as complete</ac:inline-comment-marker>, the following happens:</p><ul><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="4dee7dec-5fe0-4f29-b7ed-1446985121db">The «complete» stereotype is applied to a diagram. This way you can easily indicate complete diagrams in the model browser without opening them.</ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="4dee7dec-5fe0-4f29-b7ed-1446985121db">Auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels is suspended. This means that the diagram layout is not automatically changed when the model content changes.<br /><br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></p></li></ul><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="complete_diagram.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Complete diagrams" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The figure displays an example of a complete diagram.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="40bbb078-4d3c-4f42-af4a-076adb667cad"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Alternative</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can suspend auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels in a diagram without marking it as complete in one of the following ways:</p><ul><li>To suspend <span>auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels for individual symbols, o</span>pen the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:link-body><strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link> of a symbol or a group of symbols, and set the <strong>Suspend Auto-Resizing of Shapes</strong> and/or <strong>Suspend Auto-Displaying of Labels</strong> properties to <em>true</em>.</li><li>To suspend <span>auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels for all the diagram symbols, o</span>pen the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Specifying diagram style properties" /><ac:link-body><strong>Diagram Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link> of a diagram, and set the <strong>Suspend Auto-Resizing of Shapes</strong><span> and/or </span><strong>Suspend Auto-Displaying of Labels</strong><span> properties to </span><em>true.</em></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><br />Updating complete diagram layout</h3><p>When a diagram is marked as complete, the symbols with suspended content related to model changes are highlighted in blue (see the figure above). You can update these symbols manually, as described below.</p><p><br /></p><p>To <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="86efe6dd-ebdb-47b2-917c-0eac9ad4bc1b">update</ac:inline-comment-marker> shapes</p><hr /><ol><li>In a complete diagram, select the highlighted shape you want to update.</li><li>Do one of the following:<br /><ul><li>Drag one of the selection handles on the corner of the shape to resize it. <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="31395402-df94-4a3c-bdce-e2cbbbf321e9">The suspended content is <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="8c9350f9-dd0d-4d84-a1fc-cfc36665fee5">dynamically</ac:inline-comment-marker> displayed after resizing the shape.</ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li>Click the smart manipulator on the bottom of the shape, as shown below.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="updating_shape.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Complete diagrams" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="solver.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Complete diagrams" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> on the shape toolbar and select <strong>Resize to Fit Content</strong>.</li></ul></li></ol><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5a6d417b-8437-4169-904e-12c917ebf25a"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Updating multiple shapes</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="d9afad5e-1a0c-4f99-814d-0c31b200c81d">update multiple shapes</ac:inline-comment-marker> in a complete diagram, select the highlighted shapes you want to update and click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="resize_to_fit_content.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Complete diagrams" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> in the diagram toolbar.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To update paths</p><hr /><ol><li>In a complete diagram, select the highlighted path you want to update.</li><li><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">Do one of the following:</span><br /><ul><li>Click the smart manipulator on the path, as shown below.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="updating_path.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Complete diagrams" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>Click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="solver.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Complete diagrams" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> on the path toolbar and select <strong>Show Suspended Labels</strong>.</li></ul></li></ol><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ebb5b2fd-3e1d-43b1-9dea-cd4b4e4b35b2"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Updating multiple paths</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>To update multiple paths in a complete diagram, select the highlighted paths you want to update and click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="show_suspended_labels.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Complete diagrams" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> in the diagram toolbar.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><br />Marking diagrams as incomplete</h3><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="e19ed52e-ea35-4234-8846-966198a91fa1"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="d2f2d779-d6ba-46a3-ae59-1c0f2ca3b3f7">If</ac:inline-comment-marker> you need to resume normal work on a diagram, mark it as incomplete, as described below.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="27f48bad-1eea-4d44-96a8-a8312f1f29a3">To mark a diagram as incomplete</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><hr /><ol><li><span>Right-click a diagram on the diagram pane or in the model browser and select the </span><strong>Complete</strong><span> option to clear its check box.</span></li><li><span>When the question dialog is displayed, do one of the following:</span><ul><li><span>Select <strong>Yes</strong>, to continue suspending auto-resizing of shapes and/or auto-displaying of labels.</span></li><li>Select <strong>No</strong>, to resume auto-resizing of shapes and/or auto-displaying of labels.</li></ul></li></ol><p><br /><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="de16dfcb-8c59-4e2a-8e1c-eb4f72b31c85">If you select </ac:inline-comment-marker><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="de16dfcb-8c59-4e2a-8e1c-eb4f72b31c85">No</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="de16dfcb-8c59-4e2a-8e1c-eb4f72b31c85"> in step 2, <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">all diagram symbols are automatically resized to fit suspended content, and </span>you can continue working on the diagram as usual.</ac:inline-comment-marker> If you select <strong>Yes</strong>, auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels in the diagram are still suspended, even though the diagram is not complete.</p><h3><br />Customizing complete diagram behavior</h3><p>By default, auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels in a complete diagram are suspended. Also, the symbols with suspended content due to model changes are highlighted in blue. You can change this default behavior in the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog.</p><p><br /></p><p>To customize complete diagram behavior</p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu of your modeling tool, go to <strong>Options</strong> &gt; <strong>Project</strong>.</li><li>On the left side of the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog, expand the <strong>General</strong> node and select <strong>Diagrams</strong>.</li><li>On the right side of the dialog, specify the desired options in the <strong>Complete Diagrams</strong> group:<ul><li><strong>Suspend Auto-Resizing of Shapes</strong> - set to <em>true</em> to keep the original size of shapes while editing the model.</li><li><strong>Suspend Auto-Displaying of Labels</strong> - set to <em>true</em> if you do not want to automatically show labels in a diagram while editing the model.</li><li><strong>Highlight Suspended Symbols</strong> - set to <em>true</em> to highlight symbols that have suspended content related to model changes.</li></ul></li></ol><p><br /><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="900c833b-d566-4ac3-9fa8-00af21ec26d9">When you specify project options listed in step 3, they are applied to all diagrams that are marked as complete after specification.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><h3><strong>Check complete diagrams</strong></h3><p>To check complete diagrams</p><hr /><ul><li>In the main menu of your modeling tool, select <strong>Analyze</strong> &gt; <strong>Check Complete Diagrams</strong>.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="900c833b-d566-4ac3-9fa8-00af21ec26d9">The <strong>Check Complete Diagrams </strong>action checks for complete diagrams without completely visible content by running the following three validation rules:</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 78.296%;"><colgroup><col style="width: 20.2714%;" /><col style="width: 30.6063%;" /><col style="width: 9.77742%;" /><col style="width: 24.2493%;" /></colgroup><tbody><tr><th>Validation rule</th><th colspan="1">Error message</th><th colspan="1">Severity</th><th>Profile</th></tr><tr><td>Suspended Autodisplaying</td><td colspan="1">The symbol content is not completely visible. Auto-displaying of labels is suspended.</td><td colspan="1">warning</td><td>UML Standard Profile::Validation Profile</td></tr><tr><td>Suspended Autoresizing</td><td colspan="1">The symbol content is not completely visible. Auto-resizing of shapes is suspended.</td><td colspan="1">warning</td><td>UML Standard Profile::Validation Profile</td></tr><tr><td>Suspended Autochanges</td><td colspan="1">The diagram contains symbols whose content is not completely visible.</td><td colspan="1">warning</td><td>UML Standard Profile::Validation Profile</td></tr></tbody></table></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272738431 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 02569: (2026x) Complex Gateway

- page_id: `272738431`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272738431/2026x+Complex+Gateway
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-11-25T14:45:24.390+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) BPMN modeling > (2026x) Business Process Model and Notation Concepts > (2026x) BPMN Process Diagram > (2026x) Gateways
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1485849877

1485849879

1485849879

**Description**

A Complex Gateway can be used to model complex synchronization behavior. An Activation Condition is the Complex Gateway’s property, which is used to describe precise behavior.

**Notation**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Example**

The activation condition specifies that tokens on three out of five incoming sequence flows are needed to activate the gateway. Which token the Gateway will produce is determined by the conditions on the outgoing sequence flow as in the split behavior of an Inclusive Gateway

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Complex Gateway

1485849876

**Related elements**

- Gateways
- Sequence Flow

**Related diagrams**

- BPMN Process Diagram
- BPMN Collaboration Diagram
- BPMN Choreography Diagram

**Related procedure**

- Creating and Using a Sequence Flow

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272729939 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02570: (2026x) ComplexType

- page_id: `272729939`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272729939/2026x+ComplexType
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:36:12.082+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Data-related modeling > (2026x) XML schemas > (2026x) XML schema mapping to UML elements
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Complex type maps to UML Class with the stereotype XSDcomplexType.

- abstract - to UML Class abstract value(true | false).
- annotation - to UML Class documentation.
- attribute – to inner UML Class Attribute or UML Association End.
- attributeGroup – to UML AssociationEnd or UML Attribute with type XSDattributeGroup.
- name – to UML Class name.

This class also can have the stereotypes XSDsimpleContent, XSDcomplexContent, XSDall, XSDchoice, and XSDsequence.

No stereotype – the same as “XSDsequence”.

A generalization between a complex type and another type has the stereotype XSDrestriction or XSDextension. We assume the stereotype XSDextension if the generalizations do not have stereotypes.

Some complex mapping:

- complexType with simpleContent – to UML Class. This class must be derived from another class and must have stereotype XSDsimpleContent.
- complexType with complexContent – to UML Class. This class must be derived from another class and must have stereotype XSDcomplexContent.

#### PANEL: complexType with group, all, choice or sequence – to UML class with appropriate stereotype

complexType with group, all, choice or sequence – to UML class with appropriate stereotype

```text
<complexType
```

```text
</complexType>
```

When you choose the <simpleContent> alternative, the following elements are relevant, and the remaining property mappings are as below. Note that you must either choose <restriction> or <extension> as the content of <simpleContent>.

When the <complexContent> alternative is chosen, the following elements are relevant (as are the<attributeGroup> and <anyAttribute> elements, not repeated here), and the additional property mappings appear below. Note that you must choose either <restriction> or <extension> as the content of <complexContent>, but their content models differ in this case from the case above when they occur as children of<simpleContent>.

The property mappings below are also used in the case where the third alternative (neither <simpleContent> nor <complexContent>) is chosen. This case is understood as shorthand for complex content restricting the **ur type definition**. The details of the mappings should be modified as necessary.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Complex type maps to UML Class with the stereotype XSDcomplexType.</p><ul><li>abstract -  to UML Class abstract value(true | false).</li><li>annotation - to UML Class documentation.</li><li>attribute – to inner UML Class Attribute or UML Association End.</li><li>attributeGroup – to UML AssociationEnd or UML Attribute with type XSDattributeGroup.</li><li>name – to UML Class name.</li></ul><p>This class also can have the stereotypes XSDsimpleContent, XSDcomplexContent, XSDall, XSDchoice, and XSDsequence.</p><p>No stereotype – the same as “XSDsequence”.</p><p>A generalization between a complex type and another type has the stereotype XSDrestriction or XSDextension. We assume the stereotype XSDextension if the generalizations do not have stereotypes.</p><p>Some complex mapping:</p><ul><li>complexType with simpleContent – to UML Class. This class must be derived from another class and must have stereotype XSDsimpleContent.</li><li>complexType with complexContent – to UML Class. This class must be derived from another class and must have stereotype XSDcomplexContent.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f92f6153-0e31-448a-bb79-2e4348d3dd17"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">complexType with group, all, choice or sequence – to UML class with appropriate stereotype</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><pre>&lt;complexType</pre><pre>	abstract = boolean : false</pre><pre>	block = (#all | List of (extension | restriction))</pre><pre>	final = (#all | List of (extension | restriction)) <br />	id = ID</pre><pre>	mixed = boolean : false</pre><pre>	name = NCName</pre><pre>	{any attributes with non-schema namespace…}&gt;</pre><pre>	Content: (annotation?,(simpleContent | complexContent | ((group | al <br />((attribute | attributeGroup)*, anyAttribute?))))</pre><pre>&lt;/complexType&gt;</pre></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>When you choose the &lt;simpleContent&gt; alternative, the following elements are relevant, and the remaining property mappings are as below. Note that you must either choose &lt;restriction&gt; or &lt;extension&gt; as the content of &lt;simpleContent&gt;.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="009944e0-e6b7-4f4f-bccc-2516e12bba3c"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">&lt;restriction&gt; chosen as the content of &lt;simpleContent&gt;</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><pre>&lt;simpleContent</pre><pre>	id = ID</pre><pre>	{any attributes with non-schema namespace…}&gt; Content: (annotation?, (restriction | extension))<br />	&lt;/simpleContent&gt;</pre><pre>&lt;restriction</pre><pre>	base = OName</pre><pre>	id = ID</pre><pre>	{any attributes with non-schema namespace…}&gt;</pre><pre>	Content: (annotation?, (simpleType?, (minExclusive | minInclusive  | maxExclusive |</pre><pre>maxlnclusive | totalDigits | fractionDigits | length | minLength | maxLength</pre><pre>whitespace | pattern) *)?, ((attribute | attributeGroup)*, anyAttribute?))</pre><pre>&lt;/restriction&gt;<br />&lt;extension</pre><pre>	base = OName</pre><pre>	id = ID</pre><pre>	{any attributes with non-schema namespace…}&gt;</pre><pre>	Content: (annotation?, ((attribute | attributeGroup)*, anyAttribute?))</pre><pre>&lt;/extension&gt;</pre><pre>&lt;attributeGroup</pre><pre>	id = ID</pre><pre>	ref = OName</pre><pre>	{any attributes with non-schema namespace…}&gt;</pre><pre>	Content: (annotation?)</pre><pre>&lt;/attributeGroup&gt;</pre><pre>&lt;anyAttribute</pre></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>When the <u>&lt;complexContent&gt;</u> alternative is chosen, the following elements are relevant (as are the<u> &lt;attributeGroup&gt;</u> and <u>&lt;anyAttribute&gt;</u> elements, not repeated here), and the additional property mappings appear below. Note that you must choose either <u>&lt;restriction&gt;</u> or <u>&lt;extension&gt;</u> as the content of <u>&lt;complexContent&gt;,</u> but their content models differ in this case from the case above when they occur as children of<u> &lt;simpleContent&gt;.</u></p><p>The property mappings below are also used in the case where the third alternative (neither <u>&lt;simpleContent&gt;</u> nor <u>&lt;complexContent&gt;)</u> is chosen. This case is understood as shorthand for complex content restricting the <strong>ur type definition</strong>. The details of the mappings should be modified as necessary.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ef0542e8-9728-4810-a738-c090574a1549"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Sample of either &lt;restriction&gt; or &lt;extension&gt; must be chosen as the content of &lt;complexContent&gt;</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><pre>&lt;complexContent</pre><pre>	id = ID</pre><pre>	mixed = boolean</pre><pre>	{any attributes with non-schema namespace…}&gt;<br />	Content: (annotation?, (restriction | extension))</pre><pre>&lt;/complexContent&gt;</pre><pre>&lt;restriction</pre><pre>	base = OName</pre><pre>	id = ID</pre><pre>	{any attributes with non-schema namespace…}&gt;</pre><pre>	Content: (annotation?, (group | all | choice | sequence)?, ((attribute | attributeGroup)*, anyAttribute?))</pre><pre>&lt;/restriction&gt;</pre><pre>&lt;extension</pre><pre>	base = OName</pre><pre>	id = ID</pre><pre>	{any attributes with non-schema namespace…}&gt;</pre><pre>	Content: (annotation?, ((group | all | choice | sequence)?, ((attribute | attributeGroup)*, anyAttribute?)))</pre><pre>&lt;/extension&gt;</pre></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Figure 58 complexType UML Model Example.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) ComplexType" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>Example of complexType UML model.</h6><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1b502a09-1a14-42cb-bc44-e3052fedd33f"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Example of complexType UML model with associated XML</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><pre>&lt;?xml version=’1.0’ encoding = ‘Cp1252’?›</pre><pre>&lt;xs:schema xmlns:nm = &quot;<a href="http://nomagic.com">http://nomagic.com</a>&quot; xmlns:xs = &quot;<a href="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema">http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema</a>&quot; targetNamespace = &quot;<a href="http://nomagic.com">http://nomagic.com</a>&quot; &gt;</pre><pre>	&lt;xs:complexType name = &quot;my_Type2&quot; block = &quot;extension&quot; final = &quot;extension&quot; mixed = &quot;true&quot; &gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;xs:annotation &gt;</pre><pre>			&lt;xs:documentation &gt;my_type2</pre><pre>Documentation&lt;/xs:documentation&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;/xs:annotation&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;xs:complexContent id = &quot;contentID&quot; mixed = &quot;false&quot; &gt;<br />			&lt;xs:extension base = &quot;nm:my_Type&quot;&gt;</pre><pre>				&lt;xs:attribute name = &quot;surname&quot; type = &quot;xs:string&quot; /&gt;</pre><pre>			&lt;/xs:extension&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;/xs:complexContent&gt; <br />	&lt;/xs:complexType&gt;</pre><pre>	&lt;xs:complexType name = &quot;my_Type3&quot; &gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;xs:complexContent &gt;</pre><pre>			&lt;xs:restriction base = &quot;nm:my_Type&quot;&gt;</pre><pre>				&lt;xs:all &gt;</pre><pre>					&lt;xs:element name = &quot;order&quot; type = &quot;xs:string&quot;/&gt;</pre><pre>					&lt;xs:element name = &quot;order1&quot; type = &quot;xs:string&quot;/&gt;</pre><pre>				&lt;/xs:all&gt; <br />			&lt;/xs:restriction&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;/xs:complexContent&gt;<br />	&lt;/xs:complexType&gt;</pre><pre>	&lt;xs:complexType name = &quot;my_Type4&quot;&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;xs:simpleContent &gt;</pre><pre>			&lt;xs:restriction base = &quot;xs:string&quot; &gt;</pre><pre>				&lt;xs:minLength value = &quot;2&quot; /&gt;</pre><pre>			&lt;/xs:restriction&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;/xs:simpleContent&gt; &lt;/xs:complexType&gt;</pre><pre>	&lt;xs:complexType name = &quot;my_Type5&quot;&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;xs:simpleContent &gt;</pre><pre>			&lt;xs:extension base = &quot;xs:string&quot;&gt;</pre><pre>				&lt;xs:attribute name = &quot;attri&quot; type = &quot;xs:string&quot;/&gt;</pre><pre>			&lt;/xs:extension&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;/xs:simpleContent&gt;<br />	&lt;/xs:complexType&gt;</pre><pre>	&lt;xs:complexType name = &quot;my_Type&quot; abstract = &quot;true&quot; block = &quot;extension&quot; final = &quot;extension&quot; id = &quot;myTypeID&quot; mixed = &quot;true&quot; &gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;xs:annotation &gt;</pre><pre>			&lt;xs:documentation &gt;my_type documentation&lt;/xs:documentation&gt;<br />		&lt;/xs:annotation&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;xs:attribute name = &quot;name&quot; type = &quot;xs:string&quot; /&gt;<br />		&lt;xs:attributeGroup ref = &quot;nm:attr_group&quot; /&gt;</pre><pre>		&lt;xs:anyAttribute/&gt;<br />	&lt;/xs:complexType&gt;</pre><pre>	&lt;xs:attributeGroup name = &quot;attr_group&quot; /&gt;</pre><pre>&lt;/xs:schema&gt;</pre></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272732348 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02571: (2026x) Component Behavior

- page_id: `272732348`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272732348/2026x+Component+Behavior
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:40:28.775+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x) Exporting to External Simulation Models > (2026x) Sample models > (2026x) Creating a simple model
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1553808215

1553808216

1553808216

Now that the variables and parameters are defined, the behavior of each block can be specified. To do so, equations are used to establish the relationships among different properties.

In SysML v1, these equations are represented as constraints within *ConstraintBlocks*. Their parameters correspond to the *PhSVariables* and *PhSConstant*of the blocks (“i”, “v”, “r”) as well as the*PhSVariables*present in the type of the ports (“pv”, “pi”, “nv”, “ni”). Refer to the image below to see how these constraints are represented in a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SysML Block Definition Diagram' space=''].

An abstract ConstraintBlock *TwoPinComponentConstraint* is created to gather parameters and equations common to the source and the resistor. These equations state that the voltage of the component is the difference between the voltage in the positive pin and the voltage in the negative pin. The current of the component is the current coming from the positive pin, and the current in the two pins must add up to zero. As for the ground, the constraint states that the voltage in the pin is zero.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Constraint Blocks with specified parameters and equations in BDD.

1553808214

**Related page:**

****

To do so, equations are used to establish the relationships among different properties.</p><p>In SysML v1, these equations are represented as constraints within <em>ConstraintBlocks</em>. Their parameters correspond to the <em>PhSVariables</em> and <em>PhSConstant  </em>of the blocks (“i”, “v”, “r”) as well as the<em> PhSVariables </em>present in the type of the ports (“pv”, “pi”, “nv”, “ni”). Refer to the image below to see how these constraints are represented in a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SysML Block Definition Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Block Definition Diagram (BDD)]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p>An abstract ConstraintBlock <em>TwoPinComponentConstraint</em> is created to gather parameters and equations common to the source and the resistor. These equations state that the voltage of the component is the difference between the voltage in the positive pin and the voltage in the negative pin. The current of the component is the current coming from the positive pin, and the current in the two pins must add up to zero. As for the ground, the constraint states that the voltage in the pin is zero.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="constraints.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Component Behavior" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Constraint Blocks with specified parameters and equations in BDD.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="22bcc84a-0ee4-41c2-8c44-097a819f60f0"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1553808214</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related page:</strong></p><p><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="d092f157-fedc-46f0-ab33-04ec2fc4b642" /><br /></strong></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272733605 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02572: (2026x) Component diagram

- page_id: `272733605`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272733605/2026x+Component+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:42:36.703+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Diagrams > (2026x) UML diagrams
- labels: ['implementation', 'implementation-diagram', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

308582438

308582440

308582439

**Overview**

A Component diagram falls under the structural diagramming family.A Component diagram represents pieces of software in the implementation environment in terms of code components.

A [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Component' space='MED'] contains information about the logical [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MED'] or Classes that it implements, thus creating a mapping from a logical view to a component view. Dependencies between the Components make it easy to analyze how a change in one component affects the others. The Components may also be shown with any of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface' space='MED'] that they expose. They, as with almost any other model elements, can be grouped into [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='MED'], much like Classes or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case' space='MED'].

**Purpose**

A Component diagram models the implementation view. It is used in the later phases of the software development when there is a need to divide up Classes among different Components. When working with the CASE facilities, the Components are used for file-class mapping during code generation, reverse engineering, and round-trip engineering operations.****

**Usage**

A Component diagram can be used to:

- analyze system components and how they relate
- analyze system's physical structure
- understand the exact service behavior provided by software.

**Summary**

Component diagrams are valuable because they:

- reveal possible software configuration issues
- provide an accurate picture of existing systems prior to making changes/enhancements
- model the real software in the implementation environment
- help to reveal bottlenecks in an implementation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of a Component diagram

308582437

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating diagrams' space='']

Dependencies between the Components make it easy to analyze how a change in one component affects the others. The Components may also be shown with any of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Interfaces]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> that they expose. They, as with almost any other model elements, can be grouped into <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Package" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Packages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, much like Classes or <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Use Case" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Use Cases]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><strong>Purpose</strong></p><p>A Component diagram models the implementation view. It is used in the later phases of the software development when there is a need to divide up Classes among different Components. When working with the CASE facilities, the Components are used for file-class mapping during code generation, reverse engineering, and round-trip engineering operations.<strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Usage</strong></p><p>A Component diagram can be used to:</p><ul><li>analyze system components and how they relate</li><li>analyze system's physical structure</li><li>understand the exact service behavior provided by software. </li></ul><p><strong>Summary</strong></p><p>Component diagrams are valuable because they:</p><ul><li>reveal possible software configuration issues</li><li>provide an accurate picture of existing systems prior to making changes/enhancements</li><li>model the real software in the implementation environment</li><li>help to reveal bottlenecks in an implementation. <br /><br /></li></ul><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="component_diagram_example.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Component diagram" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of a Component diagram</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="08d36afe-7d48-42c1-b72e-10091cb4c272"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308582437</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating diagrams" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272732353 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02573: (2026x) Component variables and parameters

- page_id: `272732353`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272732353/2026x+Component+variables+and+parameters
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:40:29.325+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) SysML v1 modeling > (2026x) Exporting to External Simulation Models > (2026x) Sample models > (2026x) Creating a simple model
- labels: ['mcse', 'msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Each of the electrical component has variables corresponding to the domain being modeled. Electrical components have *voltage* and *current*. The voltage corresponds to the difference of potential between the positive and negative pin (across the component), whereas the current corresponds to the amount of charge going through the component.

Two properties *v* and*i*are created in*TwoPinComponent*. During the simulation, the voltage and the current will vary over time. As a result, the properties need to be stereotyped by *PhSVariable*. Their type must be a *ValueType* that has a unit with a symbol. Voltage and Current are already provided by the simulation library, so they can be selected (remove filters if necessary). The resistor has an additional property - the resistance. A property *r* is created in the resistor. Since the resistance will not change during the simulation, the property needs to be stereotyped by *PhSConstant.* It is necessary to create a new*ValueType* called *Resistance*that will serve as a type for this property. It must have a corresponding SysML *Unit* with a symbol set to *Ω*. Additionally, it must be a specialization of SysML*Real*.

The image below shows how [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) SysML Block Definition Diagram' space=''] should look like at this point.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The *i* and *v* Value properties typed by *Current* and *Voltage* value types.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Each of the electrical component has variables corresponding to the domain being modeled. Electrical components have <em>voltage</em> and <em>current</em>. The voltage corresponds to the difference of potential between the positive and negative pin (across the component), whereas the current corresponds to the amount of charge going through the component.</p><p>Two properties <em>v</em> and<em> i  </em>are created in<em> TwoPinComponent</em>. During the simulation, the voltage and the current will vary over time. As a result, the properties need to be stereotyped by <em>PhSVariable</em>. Their type must be a <em>ValueType</em> that has a unit with a symbol. Voltage and Current are already provided by the simulation library, so they can be selected (remove filters if necessary). The resistor has an additional property - the resistance. A property <em>r</em> is created in the resistor. Since the resistance will not change during the simulation, the property needs to be stereotyped by <em>PhSConstant.</em> It is necessary to create a new<em> ValueType</em> called <em>Resistance </em>that will serve as a type for this property. It must have a corresponding SysML <em>Unit</em> with a symbol set to <em>Ω</em>. Additionally, it must be a specialization of SysML<em> Real</em>.</p><p>The image below shows how <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) SysML Block Definition Diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Block Definition Diagram (BDD)]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> should look like at this point.</p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="component_variables_and_parameters.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Component variables and parameters" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span style="color: rgb(94,108,132);">The <em>i</em> and <em>v</em> Value properties typed by <em>Current</em> and <em>Voltage</em> value types.</span></h6><p />
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272732725 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 02575: (2026x) Composite Structure diagram

- page_id: `272732725`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272732725/2026x+Composite+Structure+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-11-25T13:41:11.839+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Diagrams > (2026x) UML diagrams
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

308768822

308768824

308768823

**Overview**

A Composite Structure diagram falls under the structural diagramming family.The Composite Structure diagram allows a decomposition and modeling of the internal structure of the classifiers, such as [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MED'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Component' space='MED'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Node' space='MED'], and other elements that are able to own other elements.

**Purpose**

A Composite Structure diagram can be used to visually represent the parts of a class, component, or collaboration, including the interaction points (called ports) that are used to access features of the structure. Additionally, Composite Structure diagrams model collaborations that describe behavior, resources used to perform the behavior, and the roles the participating resources take during the behavior.

**Usage**

Composite Structure diagram can be used to:

- analyze an object to see exactly what it is composed of
- detail internal actions of a class, including the relationships of nested classes
- show objects that need to be defined as a composition of other classified objects.

**Summary**

Composite Structure diagrams are valuable because they:

- show complex components design
- show components interfaces separately from their structures
- describe the roles played by elements in the structure.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of a Composite Structure diagram

308768820

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Creating diagrams' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ab92cd2a-a401-4844-9c5f-0ff217a1862b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308768822</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="78822c8e-9a3a-404d-ba10-fa0174493722"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308768824</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e9c673fb-cb43-494f-9b74-9ad733a6128b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308768823</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Overview</strong></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">A Composite Structure diagram falls under the structural diagramming family. </span>The Composite Structure diagram allows a decomposition and modeling of the internal structure of the classifiers, such as <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Class" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[classes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Component" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[components]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MED" ri:content-title="Node" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[nodes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, and other elements that are able to own other elements.</p><p><strong>Purpose</strong></p><p>A Composite Structure diagram can be used to visually represent the parts of a class, component, or collaboration, including the interaction points (called ports) that are used to access features of the structure. Additionally, Composite Structure diagrams model collaborations that describe behavior, resources used to perform the behavior, and the roles the participating resources take during the behavior. </p><p><strong>Usage</strong></p><p>Composite Structure diagram can be used to:</p><ul><li>analyze an object to see exactly what it is composed of</li><li>detail internal actions of a class, including the relationships of nested classes</li><li>show objects that need to be defined as a composition of other classified objects.</li></ul><p><strong>Summary</strong></p><p>Composite Structure diagrams are valuable because they:</p><ul><li>show complex components design</li><li>show components interfaces separately from their structures</li><li>describe the roles played by elements in the structure.</li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="composite_structure_diagram_example.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Composite Structure diagram" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of a Composite Structure diagram</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e03f3fe1-a2a8-47af-9d4e-3024c4e6339a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308768820</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="(2026x) Creating diagrams" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="42f4595c-1db3-4b0e-a549-bdd8f01b65b3" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=272732733 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 02576: (2026x) Composite Structure Diagram context

- page_id: `272732733`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/272732733/2026x+Composite+Structure+Diagram+context
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-11-25T14:42:13.605+01:00`
- ancestors: Versions of Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) Modeling Tools Documentation > (2026x) General modeling features > (2026x) Diagrams > (2026x) UML diagrams > (2026x) Composite Structure diagram
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

When creating a new [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Composite Structure diagram' space=''] for the element that cannot be the context of this diagram (e.g. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='MED']), the new context element (which is a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='MED']) is created automatically.

INLINE

##### Turning off automatic creation of a diagram context element

To turn off automatic creation of a diagram context element

1. On the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Understanding the user interface' space=''] , select Project > Options .
2. In the Project Options dialog, find the Diagram Context option.
3. Select the **Do Not Create** value.
4. Click OK . The automatic creation of a diagram context element is turned off.

This means that when youcreate a newdiagramforan element which cannot be the context of this diagram (e.g. [Package](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MT/(2026x) Package)),the**Select Diagram Context**dialog will open automatically.It allows you to select an existing element as a context, create a new context element by enabling the**Creation Mode**, or click the**Cancel**button if you do not want to specify the diagram context. The diagram will be created in the Package.

If you want to enable the automatic creation of a diagram context element, select the **Create Automatically** value for the **Diagram Context** option.

##### Changing the diagram context element

After the diagram is created, you can change its context element.

To change the diagram context element

1. Right-click the diagram and select Specification .
2. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='(2026x) Specification window' space='']Specification window , find the Context property.
3. Click on its value cell, and select [ATTACHMENT filename='edit_button_in_specification_window.png'] .
4. In the **Select Diagram Context** dialog, do one of the following: 
 - Select an existing element as a context in the **Tree** or **List** tab 
 - Create a new context element by enabling the **Creation Mode**>**Create**and select it.
5. Click **OK**. The diagram context element is changed.
